notes_head: The Office of the Governor of Indiana is the chief executive office of the state under Article 5 of the Indiana Constitution. The current Governor is Mike Braun, who delivered his first State of the State Address on January 29, 2025. The office operates from the Indiana Statehouse in Indianapolis and coordinates directly with the Indiana Lieutenant Governor. According to company data, the Office of the Governor of Indiana employs 14 people and is classified as a Government Administration organization.

name: United Nations
notes_head: The United Nations is an international organization founded in 1945, currently made up of 193 Member States, with work guided by the purposes and principles contained in its founding Charter. The UN has established a High-Level Advisory Body on AI to analyze the current situation and recommend governance approaches for artificial intelligence. The General Assembly approved a $3.72 billion budget for the United Nations for 2025. The UN Charter was signed on 26 June 1945 in San Francisco and came into force on 24 October, which is now recognized as UN Day.

name: European Union
notes_head: The European Union is a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe. The EU was established, along with its citizenship, when the Maastricht Treaty came into force in 1993, and was incorporated as an international legal juridical person upon entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon in 2009. In 2012, the EU was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. The European Union has enacted the Artificial Intelligence Act, which is a European Union regulation concerning artificial intelligence that entered into force on 1 August 2024.

name: DARPA
notes_head: DARPA is a research and development agency of the United States Department of Defense responsible for the development of emerging technologies for use by the military. The agency was created on February 7, 1958, by President Dwight D. Eisenhower in response to the Soviet's launch of Sputnik 1 in 1957. DARPA comprises about 220 government employees in six technical offices, including nearly 100 program managers. The agency's annual budget is $4.122 billion (FY2024).

name: U.S. Department of Transportation
notes_head: The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) is committed to safety and innovation and sees artificial intelligence (AI) as a promising capability to help achieve these aims. The Department has established an AI Compliance Plan in response to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Memorandum M-25-21, Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust. The Department was authorized in October 1966 and launched on 1 April 1967, with a mission to ensure that federal funds were effectively used to support the national transportation program. The mission of the USDOT

name: Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee
notes_head: The Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee is a standing committee of the United States Senate composed of 28 Senators and led by Committee Chairman Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Ranking Member Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.). The committee has broad jurisdiction over all matters concerning interstate commerce, science and technology policy, and transportation, and is one of the largest of the Senate's standing committees. The committee has six subcommittees which together oversee issues ranging from communications, highways, aviation, rail, shipping, transportation security, merchant marine

name: DAF-MIT AI Accelerator
notes_head: Joint partnership between the Department of the Air Force (DAF) and MIT to advance AI research for defense and civilian applications. Established 2019; renewed for another five years in 2024. Combines military personnel (embedded officers and enlisted Airmen) with MIT faculty, researchers, and students. Has launched 20+ projects engaging 150+ researchers across MIT campus and MIT Lincoln Laboratory. Focus areas include rapid prototyping, scaling, and ethical application of AI algorithms for national security. Leverages universities, small businesses, traditional defense contractors, and non-tr

name: Global AI Frontier Lab
notes_head: Joint U.S.-South Korea AI research center at NYU, established May 2024 via MOU between NYU and Korea's IITP. Co-directed by Yann LeCun and Kyunghyun Cho. Focuses on three pillars: fundamental AI research, trustworthy/responsible AI, and AI for healthcare. Korean government investing ~$34M (45B won) with NYU contributing ~$31M by 2028. Opened September 2024 at One Metrotech Center, Brooklyn.

name: Institute for Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation
notes_head: South Korean government institution under the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) that manages national ICT R&D policy, funding, and human resource development. Key funder of the Global AI Frontier Lab at NYU. Restructured in 2026 around "AI Transformation (AX)" with new teams for Physical AI, AI Foundation (data), and AI Safety and Trust. Manages program areas in AI, AI semiconductors, AX convergence, and physical AI.

name: Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation
notes_head: Key Senate committee with primary jurisdiction over AI legislation, chaired by Ted Cruz (R-TX) since 2025. Cruz unveiled the SANDBOX Act in September 2025 and a five-pillar AI policy framework explicitly advocating a light-touch, pro-innovation regulatory approach to maintain U.S. leadership over China.

name: House Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
notes_head: Bipartisan U.S. House task force created in Feb 2024 by Speaker Johnson and Leader Jeffries, co-chaired by Reps. Obernolte and Lieu. Released a 273-page final report in Dec 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations across 15 policy areas. Sunset at end of 118th Congress; no successor body established in the 119th Congress as of early 2025.

name: House Committee on Science Space & Tech
notes_head: U.S. House committee with primary jurisdiction over AI R&D policy, NIST AI standards, and NSF AI programs. Passed nine bipartisan AI bills in Sept 2024 including the CREATE AI Act (NAIRR), NSF AI Education Act, and Workforce for AI Trust Act. Contributed to the Bipartisan AI Task Force report (Dec 2024) with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. Held "Advancing America's AI Action Plan" hearings in 2025-26 with OSTP Director Michael Kratsios. Chaired by Jay Obernolte (R-CA) in 119th Congress.

name: Education and Workforce Committee
notes_head: U.S. House committee with jurisdiction over labor, education, and workforce training policy as it intersects with AI. Launched "Building an AI-Ready America" hearing series in Jan 2026 covering AI in the workplace, workplace safety, AI in education/teaching, and employer-led AI training. Examining AI's impact on hiring, gig work, and worker management. Key forum for workforce displacement and AI readiness policy.

name: International Trade Administration
notes_head: Bureau within U.S. Dept of Commerce responsible for trade promotion and commercial diplomacy. Leads the American AI Exports Program (per Trump July 2025 EO) to promote export of U.S. AI technology stack globally. Establishing National AI Center as gateway for U.S. AI exports. Has a dedicated AI Team in the U.S. Commercial Service. Accepting proposals from industry consortia (April-June 2026) for full-stack AI technology packages to international partners. Led by Under Secretary William Kimmitt.

name: U.S. Department of State
notes_head: U.S. foreign affairs agency with growing AI diplomacy role. Released first Enterprise AI Strategy FY 2024-2025 under Sec. Blinken. Key AI-relevant offices: Office of Special Envoy for Critical and Emerging Technology (S/TECH) for strategic tech competition and allied coordination; Bureau for Cyberspace and Digital Policy (CDP) for AI governance norms. Launched Partnership for Global Inclusivity on AI (PGIAI, Sept 2024) with major AI companies. Deploying internal AI tools including StateChat and agentic AI pilots. March 2026 EO by Newsom is state-level; this is federal.

name: Government Operations Agency
notes_head: California state agency (GovOps) overseeing CDT, Office of Data and Innovation, DGS, and CalHR. Leads GenAI implementation across CA state government per Newsom's 2023 executive order. Spearheading CalCompute, a public computing cluster for safe/ethical AI research. Coordinating AI procurement standards under March 2026 EO (N-5-26) requiring vendor trust/safety certifications. Houses the State Technology Council and AI Community of Practice for sharing AI best practices across departments.

name: California Department of General Services
notes_head: California state department under GovOps responsible for procurement, real estate, and administrative services. Key role in AI governance: directed by March 2026 EO (N-5-26) to develop new vendor certification requirements for AI companies seeking state contracts, requiring attestations on content safety (CSAM prevention), bias mitigation, and civil rights/liberties protections. Works with CDT to set AI procurement standards and facilitate employee access to vetted GenAI tools. Recommendations for new AI vendor certifications due within 120 days of EO.

name: California Department of Technology
notes_head: California's central IT agency (CDT), oversees state technology policy and services. Leads AI integration for state government under Gov. Newsom's executive orders (EO N-12-23). Launched 'Poppy,' a GenAI digital assistant for state employees built on large language models, with 20+ state departments testing it. Collaborates with Office of Data and Innovation (ODI) and CalHR on AI workforce training. Implements the state's Acceptable Use of AI policy for government agencies. California enacted 18 AI-related laws in 2024 and signed SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) in 2025. In March 2026,

name: California Department of Human Resources
notes_head: CalHR manages California's state workforce (civil service rules, benefits, training). Partnered with the Office of Data and Innovation (ODI) to launch the Foundations of GenAI Certificate series, a free training pathway for state employees to develop responsible GenAI skills. Part of a multi-agency AI workforce effort directed by Gov. Newsom's EO N-12-23, alongside CDT and GovOps. Also involved in creating the Chief Data and AI Officer position at Caltrans. California's Civil Rights Council approved regulations (effective Oct 2025) protecting against AI-related employment discrimination.

name: NYS Office of Information Technology Services
notes_head: New York State's central IT agency (ITS). Authored the state's 'Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence Technologies' policy (Jan 2024) under Gov. Hochul's direction, requiring leadership approval for AI systems, human oversight for automated decisions, and NIST AI RMF-based risk assessments. Appointed Eleonore Fournier-Tombs as Chief AI Officer to refine policy. Part of the Empire AI initiative. A 2025 State Comptroller audit found gaps: the policy lacks detailed implementation guidance and ITS has no inventory of AI systems across state agencies. Delivered an AI training tool for the state

name: Ohio Department of Job and Family Services
notes_head: Ohio DJFS administers unemployment insurance, workforce development, and public assistance programs. Won the 2025 Merrill Baumgardner Award from the National Association of State Workforce Agencies for AI innovation in unemployment services. Deployed four AI tools: a multilingual virtual assistant for unemployment claims (15 languages), intelligent document processing for classification/indexing, a call center AI bot, and a GenAI tool simplifying complex policy manuals. Part of Ohio's broader AI strategy coordinated through InnovateOhio. Also involved in the OhioMeansJobs AI-powered job matchi

name: Trump administration
notes_head: The Trump administration (second term, 2025-) has pursued an aggressive pro-innovation, deregulatory AI agenda. Day 1: revoked Biden's AI EO 14110 (EO 14148, Jan 20 2025). EO 14179 (Jan 23 2025) 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in AI' launched an action plan for global AI dominance. OSTP released 90-point AI Action Plan (July 2025) with three pillars: Accelerating Innovation, Building AI Infrastructure, Leading International Diplomacy/Security. Dec 2025 EO 'Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI' attempted to preempt state AI regulation, threatening to withhold federal funds from

name: Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council
notes_head: The Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC) is the UK's main public funder for engineering and physical sciences research, operating as part of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI). Major AI funding programs: Fundamental AI Research Lab (up to £40M over 5 years for novel AI research not pursued by commercial labs); AI Hubs for Science, Engineering and Real Data (£60M+ for up to nine research hubs); Turing AI Fellowships (£46M to attract/retain world-leading AI talent, delivered with Office for AI and Alan Turing Institute); Trustworthy Autonomous Systems programme (£33.7M); AI

name: U.S. National Science Foundation
notes_head: Federal agency funding fundamental AI research and workforce development. Leads the NAIRR pilot connecting 600+ research teams to national AI infrastructure and invested $100M in National AI Research Institutes in 2025. Mission reoriented by Trump administration toward AI and quantum as top priorities.

name: Pentagon
notes_head: POTENTIAL DUPLICATE: Entity 1420 (Department of Defense) already exists and is enriched. "Pentagon" is a colloquial name for the Department of Defense / its headquarters. Recommend merging edges from this entity into 1420 and deprecating this entry. Keeping minimal enrichment for reference. The Pentagon houses DoD leadership and the Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO), which has contracts with Anthropic, xAI, OpenAI, and Google. The GenAI.mil platform aims to deliver commercial AI to 3M employees. Secretary Hegseth's "AI-first" strategy directs deploying frontier models within 30 days of releas

name: U.S. Army
notes_head: The U.S. Army is actively integrating AI across operations as part of DoD's broader AI-first strategy. In December 2025, established a new 49B AI/ML Officer career field — the first dedicated AI career path for Army officers, with initial selections beginning January 2026. Defense Secretary Hegseth directed AI-driven command and control at theater/corps/division HQs by 2027, unmanned systems across every division by end 2026, and counter-UAS at platoon level by 2026. The Army is deploying AI-enabled "edge" tools for units operating on imperfect networks. Army intelligence is weaving AI into ev

name: International Telecommunication Union
notes_head: UN specialized agency for information and communication technologies. Hosts annual AI for Good Global Summit. Co-organized 2025 International AI Standards Summit with ISO and IEC, issuing the Seoul Statement on AI standards. Published 170-page report on global AI standardization landscape (2025). Develops technical standards for AI interoperability, safety, and trustworthiness. Leads initiatives on agentic AI protocols and AI in health/agriculture.

name: UNESCO
notes_head: UN agency that adopted the first global standard on AI ethics -- the Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021), applicable to all 194 member states. Developed after 3 years of consultation with experts from 155 countries. Framework centers on human rights, transparency, fairness, and human oversight. Published 2025 AI ethics toolkit with open-source NLP kits for emerging markets. Released guidelines for AI use in courts and tribunals (Dec 2025).

name: European Artificial Intelligence Board
notes_head: Advisory body established under the EU AI Act, composed of one representative from each EU Member State. Advises and assists the European Commission and Member States to facilitate consistent and effective application of the AI Act. The AI Act's phased implementation began Feb 2025 (prohibited practices), continued Aug 2025 (GPAI model obligations), with full applicability by Aug 2026. The Board operates alongside the European AI Office which supervises and enforces the Act.

name: ARPA-H
notes_head: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health, established 2022 under the Biden administration within HHS. Modeled on DARPA. Funds high-risk, high-reward health research including multiple AI programs: ADVOCATE (agentic AI for cardiovascular care), PRECISE-AI (monitoring clinical AI model degradation), TARGET (generative AI for antibiotic discovery), MOCS (AI for obstetric care), and Biomedical Data Fabric toolbox. Co-sponsors the AIxCC with DARPA for health cybersecurity.

name: Office of Management and Budget
notes_head: Executive Office of the President body that sets federal AI policy for agencies. Biden-era M-24-10 was rescinded and replaced by M-25-21 (April 2025), which mandates Chief AI Officers, AI governance boards, and risk management for high-impact AI while emphasizing innovation over restriction.

name: NASA Ames Research Center
notes_head: Major NASA research center in Silicon Valley. Intelligent Systems Division comprises Autonomous Systems & Robotics, Collaborative & Assistant Systems, Discovery & Systems Health, and Robust Software Engineering. Conducts mission-driven R&D in computational sciences for NASA applications. Aviation Systems Division researches advanced air traffic management and automation. Key AI projects: Data and Reasoning Fabric (aerospace decision-making), RAM-AO Working Group (autonomous multi-aircraft operations). Peter Norvig served as Division Chief of Computational Sciences. NASA restricts use of genera

name: CTBT organization
notes_head: The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization (CTBTO), headquartered in Vienna, is the international body overseeing the global ban on nuclear test explosions. Operates the International Monitoring System (IMS) — 337 facilities worldwide using seismic, infrasound, hydroacoustic, and radionuclide sensors. Actively incorporates AI/ML to distinguish earthquakes from nuclear explosive tests and to process vast sensor datasets. Connected to Bayesian Logic Inc. through use of the NET-VISA probabilistic model for nuclear explosion detection. Approximately 300 staff from over 90 countries.

name: Idaho National Laboratory
notes_head: U.S. Department of Energy national laboratory leading the adaptation of AI for the nuclear energy industry. Developing AI tools to reduce costs and timeframes of designing, licensing, building, and operating nuclear facilities. Major 2025 partnerships: AWS (AI tools for nuclear projects, digital twin of small modular reactor), Microsoft (Azure AI for nuclear permitting/licensing), NVIDIA (AI for reactor design and operations). DOE selected INL as a site for AI data centers under Trump administration initiative for American AI leadership. Part of DOE Project Genesis uniting all 17 national labs

name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
notes_head: NIST develops voluntary AI standards and risk management frameworks for federal agencies and industry. Its AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) is widely adopted. In 2025-26 its AI Safety Institute was renamed the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) under the Trump administration's AI Action Plan, with a mandate to revise the AI RMF.

name: Government of Canada
notes_head: Canada was the first country globally to adopt a national AI strategy, launching the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy in 2017. Phase 2 announced in 2022 with $443M+ in funding, supporting the three national AI institutes (Mila, Amii, Vector). Tabled the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) in June 2022 as part of Bill C-27, one of the first proposed AI-specific laws globally. AIDA focuses on responsible design, development, and use of AI in the private sector, with emphasis on health, safety, and human rights impacts. Requires businesses to implement governance mechanisms, assess AI risks, and

name: OECD
notes_head: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (38 member countries). Published OECD AI Principles in 2019 (first intergovernmental AI standard), updated 2024 — now endorsed by 47 jurisdictions. Operates AI Policy Observatory (oecd.ai) tracking AI strategies, policies, and incidents globally. Integrated GPAI partnership under OECD umbrella (44 countries). Partners with UN Office of Tech Envoy. Published common AI incident reporting framework (Feb 2025). Member-funded intergovernmental org.

name: Arizona Secretary of State
notes_head: Office held by Adrian Fontes (D). Formed AI & Election Security Advisory Committee (June 2024), co-chaired by Chris Cummiskey and Gowri Ramachandran. Released final report (Feb 2025) proposing AI Elections Lab for developing guidelines and pilot programs. Deployed AI tools in 2024 election: chatbot handled 655K+ interactions, SMS system reached 281K voters. Advocates both beneficial AI use in elections and safeguards against AI-driven threats. Pushes for state and federal AI election policy.

name: U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute
notes_head: Housed within NIST. Develops science-based metrics, tools, and guidelines for AI safety evaluation. Established by Biden EO 14110 (Oct 2023). Paul Christiano served as Head of AI Safety. Runs AISIC consortium (290+ members). Renamed to Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) in 2025. Budget ~$10M (2024). NOTE: Related entities — 205 (U.S. AI Safety Institute at NIST, likely same entity), 1629 (AISIC consortium, its membership body), 1309 (NIST, parent agency).

name: UK government's Frontier AI Taskforce
notes_head: UK government body established April 2023 with £100m mandate to evaluate frontier AI safety risks ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Chaired by Ian Hogarth, it transitioned in November 2023 into the AI Safety Institute (later renamed AI Security Institute in 2025), which is a separate entity in this database.

name: Senate AI Caucus
notes_head: Bipartisan U.S. Senate caucus on artificial intelligence, launched March 2019 by Sens. Martin Heinrich (D-NM) and Rob Portman (R-OH). Original members included Schatz (D-HI), Gardner (R-CO), Peters (D-MI), Ernst (R-IA). After Portman's retirement (2023), Mike Rounds (R-SD) became co-chair. Heinrich named one of TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI. The caucus works toward responsible AI policy balancing innovation with ethical standards.

name: Department of Energy
notes_head: Federal agency overseeing U.S. energy systems and nuclear infrastructure. Plays a central AI role through its 17 National Laboratories, its membership in the TRAINS Taskforce for national security AI testing, and its initiative to co-locate AI data centers with federal energy infrastructure across 16 identified sites.

name: Small Business Administration
notes_head: U.S. federal agency (est. 1953) supporting small businesses and entrepreneurs. Increasingly relevant to AI policy as small business AI adoption surges (58% using generative AI per 2025 Chamber survey, up from 40%). Provides AI guidance for small businesses (sba.gov/ai). Subject of the bipartisan Small Business AI Training Act (2026, Cantwell/Moran) directing Commerce Dept + SBA to create AI training resources. Under Senate oversight scrutiny regarding AI practices (Sen. Ernst). Trump admin's 2026 AI policy framework includes 'AI infrastructure and small business support' pillar.

name: Department of Defense
notes_head: The Department of Defense is the largest U.S. federal agency and a major driver of AI policy and procurement. In January 2026, Secretary Hegseth released an "AI-first" strategy directing the department to adopt AI at wartime speed, including deploying frontier models to warfighters within 30 days of public release. The Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO) was realigned under the Under Secretary for R&E/CTO Emil Michael with a Barrier Removal Board to accelerate AI delivery. DoD AI work spans autonomous systems, logistics, intelligence, and enterprise operations across seven pace-setting projects

name: Joint Economic Committee
notes_head: The Joint Economic Committee (JEC) is a bicameral congressional committee that studies economic policy. In the 119th Congress (2025–2026), it is chaired by Rep. David Schweikert (R-AZ) with Sen. Maggie Hassan (D-NH) as Ranking Member. The JEC held a hearing in June 2024 on "Artificial Intelligence and Its Potential to Fuel Economic Growth and Improve Governance," examining AI's productivity impacts and regulatory approaches. The committee's AI work focuses primarily on economic competitiveness and workforce implications.

name: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
notes_head: Senate's primary oversight committee for government operations and DHS. Active AI oversight role: held hearings on 'AI Risks and Opportunities' and 'AI in Government.' Key legislation includes AI LEAD Act (Chief AI Officer at every federal agency), No Adversarial AI Act (S.2177, protecting government from foreign adversary AI), and AI procurement standards bill (Peters-Tillis). Focus on federal AI use management, security, and procurement guardrails.

name: U.S. Senate Commerce Committee
notes_head: U.S. Senate committee with primary jurisdiction over AI, technology, and telecommunications. Chaired by Ted Cruz (R-TX) since 2025, who introduced the SANDBOX Act and a five-pillar light-touch AI framework. Oversees the Senate AI Working Group and has held hearings on U.S. AI competitiveness vs. China.

name: National Artificial Intelligence Advisory Commission
notes_head: Federal advisory committee created by National AI Initiative Act of 2020. Advises the President and National AI Initiative Office (within OSTP). Housed at NIST. Members drawn from academia, nonprofits, civil society, private sector. Covers AI competitiveness, workforce, R&D, standards, ethics, governance. Finalized transition recommendations for Trump admin (Mar 2025) with 10 priority recommendations. NOTE: Formally called NAIAC (National AI Advisory Committee) — 'Commission' in entity name may be a slight misnomer.

name: U.S. Department of Labor
notes_head: Federal cabinet department overseeing labor standards, workforce, and employment. Key AI role: published national AI Literacy Framework (Feb 2026) with 5 content areas and 7 delivery principles. Partnered with NSF on AI-ready workforce initiative (MOU for AI literacy, training pathways, labor market research). Hosts AI page at dol.gov/ai. Focus on workforce preparation for AI disruption through guidance and skills development rather than heavy regulation. Daniel Ho serves as Senior Advisor on Responsible AI.

name: House Science, Space, and Technology Committee
notes_head: DUPLICATE of entity 1150 ('House Committee on Science Space & Tech'). This is the same U.S. House committee — full formal name is 'House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.' Entity 1150 is already enriched (phase3-manual). Recommend merging edges to 1150 and soft-deleting this entity. NOTE: Edge 1646 (employer: Frank Lucas -> this entity, role='Chairman') should be reassigned to entity 1150.

name: House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy
notes_head: House Democratic caucus body launched Dec 2025 by Leader Hakeem Jeffries. Co-chaired by Rep. Ted Lieu (CA-36), Rep. Josh Gottheimer (NJ-05), and Rep. Valerie Foushee (NC-04). Ex officio co-chairs: Rep. Zoe Lofgren (Ranking Member, Science, Space & Tech Committee) and Rep. Frank Pallone Jr. (Ranking Member, Energy & Commerce Committee). Will convene throughout 2026 to develop AI policy expertise on guardrails, economic impacts, safety, and health. Successor to Democrats' participation in the now-expired Bipartisan AI Task Force. All House Democratic Caucus members invited to participate.

name: Bipartisan Taskforce on Artificial Intelligence
notes_head: DUPLICATE of entity 1149 ('House Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence'). This is the same body — the bipartisan House task force created Feb 2024 by Speaker Johnson and Leader Jeffries, co-chaired by Reps. Obernolte and Lieu. Released 273-page final report Dec 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. Sunset at end of 118th Congress. Entity 1149 is already enriched (phase3-manual). Recommend merging edges to 1149 and soft-deleting this entity. NOTE: Edge 1651 (employer: Ted Lieu -> this entity, role='Co-Chair') should be reassigned to entity 1149.

name: Commerce, Science and Transportation Committee
notes_head: DUPLICATE — this is the same entity as 1148 ('Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation'), 1428 ('U.S. Senate Commerce Committee'), and 1107 ('Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee'). Recommend merging edges to 1148 (the most complete record) and soft-deleting this entity. See those entries for full enrichment.

name: Congressional Artificial Intelligence Caucus
notes_head: Bipartisan House caucus promoting AI education and policy dialogue. Originally co-chaired by Reps. Anna Eshoo (D-CA) and Michael McCaul (R-TX); Don Beyer (D-VA) was an active co-chair figure. Convenes members to engage on AI legislation, coordinates with the House AI Task Force, and maintains a nonpartisan pro-innovation stance.

name: House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
notes_head: DUPLICATE — this is the same entity as 1149 ('House Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence') and likely 1470 ('Bipartisan Taskforce on Artificial Intelligence'). The official name is 'Bipartisan House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence,' established Feb 2024 by Speaker Johnson and Leader Jeffries, co-chaired by Reps. Obernolte (R-CA) and Lieu (D-CA). Released 273-page final report Dec 2024 with 66 findings and 85 recommendations. Recommend merging edges to 1149 and soft-deleting this entity.

name: Congressional AI Caucus
notes_head: DUPLICATE — this is the same entity as 1481 ('Congressional Artificial Intelligence Caucus'). 'Congressional AI Caucus' is an informal short name for the Congressional Artificial Intelligence Caucus, the bipartisan House caucus co-founded by Reps. Eshoo and McCaul to inform policymakers on AI impacts. Recommend merging edges to 1481 and soft-deleting this entity.

name: National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Office
notes_head: Federal coordinating office established by the National AI Initiative Act of 2020, housed within the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP). Serves as central coordination hub for AI R&D across federal agencies, industry, academia, nonprofits, and state/tribal governments. Led the National AI R&D Strategic Plan updates. Four statutory mandates: ensure continued U.S. AI R&D leadership, develop trustworthy AI systems, prepare the workforce for AI integration, and coordinate federal AI activities across civilian agencies, DoD, and intelligence community. First director was Lyn

name: Empire AI
notes_head: New York State AI consortium launched January 2024 by Governor Kathy Hochul. Backed by $400M+ in public and private funds ($275M state investment, expanded to $500M+ total). Building state-of-the-art AI computing center on the University at Buffalo campus. Consortium of 10 member institutions: Columbia, Cornell, NYU, RPI, SUNY, CUNY, Simons Foundation, University of Rochester, RIT, and Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai. Focused on responsible AI research for the public good — workforce development, healthcare, climate science. Independent analysis projects billions in economic benefits f

name: US AI Safety Institute Consortium
notes_head: NIST-led consortium of 290+ companies, universities, and civil society organizations launched in February 2024 to advance AI safety research and standards. Renamed to Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) in June 2025 under the Trump administration, shifting focus from safety to innovation and national security.

name: General Services Administration
notes_head: The General Services Administration (GSA) is a federal agency that manages government buildings, procurement, and technology services. GSA plays a central role in federal AI adoption: it launched USAi in August 2025, the government's first shared platform for AI experimentation providing chat-based AI, code generation, and document summarization tools. GSA also oversees FedRAMP (including the 2025 FedRAMP 20x initiative for streamlined cloud authorization) and proposed contract clause GSAR 552.239-7001 in March 2026 requiring AI safeguards from government contractors. The agency's AI strategy

name: Department of Homeland Security
notes_head: Federal cabinet department responsible for border security, cybersecurity, immigration, and disaster response. Published a three-year AI strategy focused on responsible adoption across mission areas: threat detection, border surveillance (CBP edge AI), cybersecurity defense, and emergency response. In Jan 2025 issued DHS Directive 139-08 setting prohibited AI uses and acquisition governance. CIO serves as Chief AI Officer; AI Governance Board convened July 2025. Created AI Safety and Security Board advising critical infrastructure operators. Member of NIST TRAINS Taskforce (AI safety testing).

name: National Institutes of Health
notes_head: Primary federal agency for biomedical and public health research, part of HHS. Developing its first institute-wide AI Strategic Plan (RFI closed July 2025) covering AI in biomedical discovery, clinical decision support, and public health. AI/ML research funding grew 233% (inflation-adjusted) from FY2019–2023, outpacing overall NIH budget growth. In Aug 2025 issued policy restricting AI-generated content in grant applications (substantially AI-generated proposals disqualified; post-award AI misuse triggers misconduct investigation). Facing FY2026 budget cuts that may constrain future AI funding

name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
notes_head: UK government department responsible for AI policy, created in February 2023. Houses the UK AI Safety Institute (AISI). Published a pro-innovation AI White Paper (March 2023) establishing five cross-sectoral principles: Safety/Security/Robustness, Transparency/Explainability, Fairness, Accountability/Governance, Contestability/Redress. Relies on principles-based guidance for existing sectoral regulators rather than dedicated horizontal AI legislation. Launched the AI Opportunities Action Plan (January 2025) focused on economic growth through AI adoption. Opened consultation on AI Growth Lab (O

name: Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS)
notes_head: Commerce Department sub-agency that administers U.S. export controls under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). BIS designed and enforces the semiconductor export controls targeting China first adopted October 2022 and significantly expanded October 2023 — the primary U.S. regulatory lever on frontier-AI compute. Issues licenses, foreign-direct-product rules, and enforcement actions affecting NVIDIA, AMD, TSMC, and downstream chip consumers. Also oversees the Entity List.

name: National Security Council (NSC)
notes_head: White House body that coordinates national-security, foreign-policy, and technology-and-security policy across federal agencies. Chaired by the President; led operationally by the National Security Advisor. Hosts the Director for Technology and National Security portfolio, which coordinates AI-and-national-security policy including chip export controls, compute access, and military AI use. Originating home of the 2023 Biden AI Executive Order process.

name: President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST)
notes_head: Federal advisory committee to the President and White House on science, technology, and innovation policy. Members are external experts from academia and industry, appointed by the President. Issued the landmark April 2024 report 'Supercharging Research: Harnessing Artificial Intelligence to Meet Global Challenges', which shaped Biden-era federal AI science priorities and is a reference document for subsequent policy debates.

name: Bureau of Cyberspace and Digital Policy (CDP)
notes_head: State Department bureau established April 2022 that leads U.S. diplomacy on cybersecurity, digital freedom, and international technology governance including AI. CDP coordinates U.S. positions at the UN, G7, OECD, and Council of Europe on AI governance, and engages with allied governments on trusted-AI frameworks and standards. Headed by the U.S. Ambassador at Large for Cyberspace and Digital Policy.

name: Cloudflare
notes_head: Founded 2009, provides web infrastructure services including CDN, security, and AI tools. Offers Workers AI platform with 80+ models from various providers but doesn't train LLMs itself. Launched AI Security Suite and application confidence scoring system. Created marketplace allowing websites to charge AI bots for content access. CEO Matthew Prince actively lobbying regulators on AI crawler separation.

name: Nvidia
notes_head: Founded 1993, became dominant AI chip provider with 92% discrete GPU market share in 2025. Market cap reached $4.6 trillion. Invested in 67+ AI startups in 2025, including OpenAI ($100M), Anthropic ($10B), and xAI ($6B). CEO Jensen Huang lobbied Trump administration to approve H200 chip sales to China, facing bipartisan congressional opposition over national security concerns.

name: Qualcomm
notes_head: Founded 1985 by UC San Diego professors, semiconductor company with 30,000+ employees (20,000+ engineers). Investing in AI R&D for 15+ years, first to run Stable Diffusion on smartphone (2023). Holds 130,000+ patents, partners with IBM and Truepic on AI initiatives. Focuses on edge AI and on-device inference capabilities.

name: TSMC
notes_head: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is the world's largest contract chip manufacturer, fabricating over 90% of advanced semiconductors as of 2023. Founded 1987, manufactures chips for Nvidia, Apple, AMD and other AI companies. Revenue $122.4B in 2025, planning $56B capex in 2026. Critical supplier for AI infrastructure but not an AI research lab.

name: Huawei
notes_head: Chinese tech giant developing AI chips (Ascend series) and systems to compete with Nvidia. Launched CloudMatrix 384 AI system with 384 Ascend 910C chips in 2025. Under US export restrictions but continues advancing AI capabilities through system-level design innovations. Operates Noah's Ark Laboratory for AI research since establishment.

name: Arm
notes_head: Semiconductor IP company founded 1990, designs processor architectures used globally. Powers AI infrastructure from cloud datacenters (50% of hyperscaler compute in 2025) to edge devices. Exceeded $1 billion revenue for three consecutive quarters in 2025. Supports 22 million developers worldwide building on Arm compute platform.

name: Massachusetts Green High Performance Computing Center
notes_head: Intercollegiate high-performance computing facility in Holyoke, Massachusetts, jointly operated by Boston University, Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, UMass, and Yale. LEED Platinum certified; powered by Holyoke Gas & Electric with >90% carbon-free energy (>70% local hydro and solar). Hosts hundreds of thousands of CPUs, millions of GPU cores, >50PB storage. Announced $16M expansion in Oct 2024 to build a quantum computing complex. Houses the Kempner AI Cluster for Harvard. Connected to Holyoke's municipal fiber grid and numerous campus, regional, and national networks.

name: AMD
notes_head: Major semiconductor company competing with NVIDIA in AI accelerators. CEO Lisa Su targets 35% annual revenue growth driven by AI chip demand. Product roadmap: MI308, MI450 (Q3 2026), MI500 (2027). Key partnerships: OpenAI (multi-year, multi-billion chip deal), Meta (6GW deal). Took $800M charge from U.S. export controls on MI308 to China (Apr 2025). Su advocates balanced export policy — supports national security restrictions but warns overly strict controls hurt U.S. competitiveness.

name: Yale Center for Research Computing
notes_head: Yale University's central HPC and research computing facility (YCRC). Part of $150M AI investment initiative. Transitioning from Yale West Campus Data Center to MGHPCC (consortium with Harvard, MIT, BU, Northeastern, UMass). New Bouchet cluster in production (~4,000 cores), expanding with 80 NVIDIA H200 GPUs (140GB VRAM each), 48 A5000 ADA cards, and 6,000 additional CPUs. Grace and McCleary clusters decommissioning in 2026. Launched Hopper secure HPC cluster (Jul 2025). All new purchases including Provost's AI Initiative GPUs installed at MGHPCC.

name: National Infrastructure Fund
notes_head: Saudi Arabian infrastructure financing entity operating under the National Development Fund. Signed $1.2B financing framework with Humain (announced at Davos, Jan 2026) to develop 250MW of AI data center capacity. Exploring creation of AI data center investment platform to attract global/local institutional investors for Humain AI strategy. Supports Vision 2030 tech infrastructure goals.

name: Helion Energy
notes_head: Private nuclear fusion company building the Orion plant in Chelan County, WA, targeting power delivery to Microsoft data centers by 2028 under the world's first fusion power purchase agreement. Backed by Sam Altman ($350M+) and SoftBank; raised $425M Series F in Jan 2025 at a $5.4B valuation. Directly tied to AI compute energy demands.

name: SpaceX
notes_head: Aerospace and space launch company that merged with xAI in February 2026 to create a $1.25T 'Orbital Intelligence' entity. Plans space-based AI data centers using Starlink satellite infrastructure. Founder Elon Musk favors light-touch AI regulation while pursuing aggressive AI deployment through xAI's Grok models.

name: Neuralink
notes_head: Brain-computer interface company co-founded by Elon Musk (2016). Developing implantable BCI (N1 chip) for paralysis patients. FDA IDE approval May 2023; first human implant Jan 2024 (PRIME trial). FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for speech restoration tech (May 2025). Raised over $700M. Regulatory gap: neural data privacy laws lag behind technology. Competitor Precision Neuroscience also received FDA clearance.

name: ASML
notes_head: Dutch semiconductor equipment manufacturer holding a global monopoly on Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines, essential for manufacturing advanced AI chips at nodes below 7nm. Founded 1984, headquartered in Veldhoven, Netherlands. EUV machines cost up to $400M each. Central to U.S.-China chip export control dynamics — the Dutch government has blocked EUV exports to China since 2019 under U.S. pressure. Current CEO Christophe Fouquet (since 2024) and predecessor Peter Wennink have both opposed further export restrictions, arguing they are economically motivated and could push China to

name: Writers Guild of America
loc: Los Angeles, CA (WGAW) and New York, NY (WGAE)
notes_head: The Writers Guild of America reached a tentative four-year agreement with the Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers in 2026, finalized after roughly three weeks of negotiations. The deal addresses AI protections, compensation increases, and health plan strengthening, coming nearly three years after a 148-day strike in 2023. The Writers Guild of America is comprised of two different American labor unions representing writers in film, television, radio, and online media: the Writers Guild of America, East (WGAE) headquartered in New York City and affiliated with the AFL-CIO, and th

name: SAG-AFTRA
loc: Los Angeles, CA (with NYC office and locals nationwide)
notes_head: SAG-AFTRA represents approximately 160,000 actors, announcers, broadcast journalists, dancers, DJs, news writers, news editors, program hosts, puppeteers, recording artists, singers, stunt performers, voiceover artists and other entertainment and media professionals. SAG-AFTRA was formed in 2012 through the merger of Screen Actors Guild (founded 1933) and American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (founded 1937). The union is a proud affiliate of the AFL-CIO and has national offices in Los Angeles and New York with local offices nationwide. SAG-AFTRA has been actively negotiating AI p

name: Fairly Trained
loc: London, UK (with operations in Palo Alto, CA)
notes_head: Founded in 2024 by Ed Newton-Rex, former VP Audio at Stability AI who resigned over ethical concerns about copyright. Operates AI model certification program with 'Licensed Model' certification for companies that only use licensed training data. Has certified 20+ AI companies including Beatoven, Boomy, LifeScore, and Rightsify. Supported by Universal Music Group, SAG-AFTRA, Authors Guild, and Association of American Publishers. Organized 1000+ UK musician protest album 'Is This What We Want?' in 2025 against proposed UK copyright changes.

name: Data Workers' Inquiry
loc: Berlin, Germany (with global presence across 9 countries)
notes_head: Joint project of DAIR Institute, Weizenbaum Institut, and TU Berlin, led by Dr. Milagros Miceli. Engaged 25 data workers as community researchers across Venezuela, Kenya, Syria, Brazil, Germany, and France. Developed Workers' Inquiry as Research Methodology (WIRM). Supported creation of Data Labelers Association in Kenya and testified at European Parliament, directly influencing Platform Workers' Directive. Three community researchers named to TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI.

name: ControlAI
loc: London, UK (active in US, Canada, Germany)
notes_head: Non-profit founded by Andrea Miotti, structured as not-for-profit company in UK and 501(c)(4) in US. Has briefed 150+ UK parliamentarians since 2024, with 100+ supporting their campaign statement on AI extinction risks. Uses 'Direct Institutional Plan' (DIP) approach - directly contacting lawmakers about superintelligence risks rather than traditional lobbying. Team of ~14 people including policy advisors across multiple countries. Advisors include Connor Leahy (CEO of Conjecture) and Gabriel Alfour.

name: Stop AI
notes_head: Founded 2024 by Sam Kirchner and Guido Reichstadter after splitting from PauseAI over tactical disagreements. Known for confrontational civil disobedience including chaining OpenAI office doors shut (February arrests) and barricading company gates. Organization expelled founder Kirchner in November 2024 after alleged violent threats against OpenAI employees. Now led by Matthew Hall ('Yakko'), shifting toward less confrontational messaging while maintaining commitment to stopping AGI development.

name: High Impact Professionals
notes_head: Founded as career transition organization for experienced professionals seeking high-impact roles. Runs 6-week Impact Accelerator Program and maintains Talent Directory with 4,500+ professionals. In 2024 facilitated 20+ career transitions and inspired 20 effective giving pledges, generating estimated $1M+ counterfactual impact. Executive Director Nina Friedrich, co-founder Devon Fritz. Funded by Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving. Current 3-person team with runway through February 2025.

name: Upgradable
notes_head: Nonprofit organization helping existential safety advocates systematically optimize their lives and work through 'Upgrade Programs' (UPs). Offers coaching, consulting, and life optimization services specifically targeting AI safety researchers, entrepreneurs, and advocates. Team has built 25+ organizations operating 200+ years cumulatively, including Effective Altruism Global and EA Ventures (advocating AI safety since 2015). Provides startup templates, organizational support, and personal optimization across productivity, health, and effectiveness domains.

name: American Federation of Teachers
notes_head: Founded 1916, represents 1.8 million educators and public workers. Launched $23M National Academy for AI Instruction (2025) with Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic. Published 'Commonsense Guardrails for Using Advanced Technology in Schools' and secured first K-12 AI contract language in St. Paul. President: Randi Weingarten.

name: PauseAI
loc: Global (founded in Utrecht, Netherlands; US operations headquartered in Bay Area, California)
notes_head: Founded May 2023 by Joep Meindertsma in Netherlands. Global grassroots movement with 600+ members, chapters in 13+ countries including PauseAI US (501c3). Recent funding from Future of Life Institute. Organized protests at Google DeepMind, OpenAI, AI Safety Summits. Claims 80% public support for AI pause based on polling. Hiring policy directors and chapter leads as of 2025.

name: Probably Good
loc: San Francisco, CA (team distributed across US, UK, Belgium, Israel, Norway)
notes_head: Founded as nonprofit career guidance organization, 7 employees with 57% YoY growth. Executive Director: Itamar Shatz (PhD Cambridge). Provides 1:1 career advising, job board with 1,300+ high-impact roles, fellowships, workshops. Strong focus on AI safety/governance careers, EA community connections. Revenue model through philanthropic donations.

name: Successif
notes_head: Founded by Claire Boine (formerly Pathfinder, rebranded April 2023). Career transition organization helping mid-career professionals move into AI safety and governance roles. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($778K+ in grants). Offers AI Policy Strategy Fellowship, career mentoring, and professional training. Previously supported 40+ EA and AI safety organizations. Team includes technical advisors and policy experts.

name: The AI Whistleblower Initiative (AIWI)
loc: Berlin, Germany (with presence in UK and Kenya)
notes_head: Founded 2024 as nonprofit hosted by Whistleblower Netzwerk e.V. Staff of 7 with 200% growth. Offers 'Third Opinion' anonymous consultation service connecting AI insiders with independent experts. Launched 'Publish Your Policies' campaign with 35+ signatories demanding AI companies publish whistleblowing policies. Documented 22 whistleblower cases (6 named, 16 anonymous) in comprehensive resource. Provides legal support connections and policy advocacy in EU and US.

name: Stop AGI
notes_head: Public education website focused on communicating AGI extinction risks to general audiences. Provides detailed policy proposals including global moratoriums, compute caps, and international AI safety institutions. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map but website remains operational with recent content updates.

name: Encode Justice
notes_head: Founded by 19-year-old Sneha Revanur, youth-led organization with 1,000 members under age 25 across 30 countries. Successfully passed California's SB 53 AI safety law, New York's RAISE Act, and the TAKE IT DOWN Act against deepfake pornography. Funded by foundations including Heising-Simons, Future of Life Institute, and Archewell Foundation, explicitly refusing corporate or AI company executive funding.

name: Campaign to Stop Killer Robots
notes_head: Founded in 2012, publicly launched 2013. Global coalition of 270+ NGOs and academic partners across 70+ countries. Led campaign resulting in 30 countries calling for autonomous weapons ban and over 120 states supporting legally binding instrument. Coordinated by Human Rights Watch until 2021, now has dedicated staff team.

name: AFL-CIO
notes_head: Nation's largest labor federation representing 63 unions and nearly 15 million workers. Launched 'Workers First Initiative on AI' in October 2025 with comprehensive principles for worker-centered AI. Created AFL-CIO Technology Institute in 2021 and AI task force in 2024 to fight for state-level AI safety laws.

name: Humans First
notes_head: Nonpartisan grassroots movement originally incubated by Center for AI Safety, now independent 501(c)(3) applicant. Cross-partisan coalition includes progressive unions (AFL-CIO affiliates), conservative groups (Congress of Christian Leaders), and prominent figures like Steve Bannon, Susan Rice, Ralph Nader. Conducting nationwide town halls across red and blue states, with directors including Amy Kremer (Women for Trump co-founder) and Alexander McCoy. Key campaign: opposing 'Big AI' political donations from 11 companies including OpenAI, Anthropic, Palantir.

name: Alliance for a Better Future
notes_head: Founded March 2026 by Janet Kelly (former VA Health Secretary under Gov. Youngkin) and Tim Estes (AI CEO/tech entrepreneur). Coalition of 9 conservative organizations including Heritage Foundation, Family Policy Alliance, NCOSE. Plans to spend at least $10+ million in 2026 on advocacy campaigns. Policy council chaired by Brad Littlejohn (American Compass). Launched with polling showing 88% support for AI chatbot suicide discussion bans and company liability for harmful AI advice.

name: QuitGPT
notes_head: Consumer boycott campaign launched February 2026 targeting OpenAI's Pentagon contracts and political donations. Claims over 4 million participants based on pledges and social media engagement. Coalition includes democracy activists, climate organizers, tech workers, and pro-democracy groups. Documented measurable impact including 295% spike in ChatGPT uninstalls and competitor app growth. Core demands include ending OpenAI executive donations to Trump/Republicans and refusing military AI contracts for surveillance and autonomous weapons.

name: Amazon Labor Union
notes_head: The Amazon Labor Union (ALU) was founded by warehouse workers at the JFK8 Fulfillment Center in Staten Island, New York, and is now known as Amazon Labor Union–IBT Local 1. The NLRB recently ordered Amazon.com Services to bargain with Amazon Labor Union at a fulfillment center located in Staten Island, N.Y. (JFK8 Facility). Chris Smalls is identified as the Amazon Labor Union founder who wrote a book called 'When the Revolution Comes'. The union operates as a worker-led union committed to transforming conditions at Amazon and describes itself as a fighting union built by and for Amazon workers

name: Social Media Victims Law Center
notes_head: The Social Media Victims Law Center (SMVLC) is a legal advocacy organization supporting families harmed by predatory tech. Originally focused on social media harms to children, it has expanded into AI litigation. In November 2025, SMVLC and the Tech Justice Law Project filed seven lawsuits against OpenAI alleging wrongful death, assisted suicide, and product liability related to ChatGPT's GPT-4o model. The suits claim OpenAI knowingly released a dangerously sycophantic product that fostered psychological dependency and contributed to suicides. SMVLC has also filed lawsuits against Character.AI

name: Tech Justice Law Project
notes_head: The Tech Justice Law Project (TJLP) is a pioneering impact litigation and advocacy organization established in 2023 as a project of Campaign for Accountability. It works with legal experts, policy advocates, digital rights organizations, and technologists to ensure legal and policy frameworks are fit for the digital age. TJLP has been particularly active in AI litigation, co-filing seven lawsuits with the Social Media Victims Law Center against OpenAI in November 2025 alleging ChatGPT caused wrongful deaths through emotional manipulation and sycophantic design. Policy Counsel Melodi Dinçer foc

name: Court Watch NOLA
notes_head: Founded 2007, oldest court-watching program in the U.S. Recruits, trains, and supports volunteers to observe Orleans Parish Criminal Court judges, prosecutors, and public defenders for transparency, ethics, and constitutional compliance. Partnered with Tulane University on $1.5M NSF-funded project using AI to build a data analysis platform for criminal justice transparency -- AI extracts info from court documents, identifies trends and inequities, and directs court watchers to high-priority cases. Dashboard tracking bail settings launched Dec 2024; full AI platform expected early 2026.

name: Black in AI
notes_head: Nonprofit founded 2017 by Timnit Gebru and Rediet Abebe. Increases presence and inclusion of Black people in AI. Four pillars: education pathways, financial barriers, visibility, and community support. Received MacArthur Foundation funding. Hosts annual workshop at NeurIPS. Aims to broaden voices in AI development, deployment, and regulation.

name: AI4ALL
notes_head: Nonprofit co-founded in 2017 by Fei-Fei Li, Olga Russakovsky, and Rick Sommer to increase diversity in AI. Grew from 2015 Stanford summer program for high school girls. 2022 strategic pivot to College Pathways — accelerating underrepresented college students into AI internships and research. Partnerships with 16+ universities (Texas A&M, UIUC, UMBC). Early funders include Melinda French Gates/Pivotal Ventures and Jensen Huang.

name: Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers
notes_head: Trade association representing major Hollywood studios and streaming platforms (Disney, Netflix, Warner Bros. Discovery, NBCUniversal, Paramount, Sony, Amazon/MGM, Apple) in collective bargaining with entertainment unions. Central player in 2023 SAG-AFTRA and WGA strikes where AI was a key issue. Agreed to: require advance written consent from performers to create and use digital replicas (AI-generated likenesses); prohibit requiring writers to use AI; disclose AI involvement in scriptwriting. IATSE contract ratified in 2024 despite union concerns about insufficient AI protections. Committed t

name: Tarbell Center for AI Journalism
loc: Claymont, DE (with global team in US and UK)
notes_head: The Tarbell Center for AI Journalism supports journalism that helps society navigate the emergence of increasingly advanced AI. The organization offers grants of $1,000–$20,000 to support original reporting published in established outlets, whether from freelancers or staff. The Tarbell Fellowship is a one-year program for journalists interested in covering artificial intelligence, with fellows securing a nine-month placement at a major newsroom and participating in a 10-week course covering AI and journalism fundamentals. Editorial independence is a core value for Tarbell and their grantees m

name: The Verge
notes_head: The Verge is about technology and how it makes us feel, founded in 2011. The Verge was founded in 2011 in partnership with Vox Media, and covers the intersection of technology, science, art, and culture. The Verge is an ambitious multimedia effort to examine how technology will change life in the future for a massive mainstream audience. The site is powered by Vox Media's Chorus platform, a modern media stack built for web-native news in the 21st century.

name: Transformer (newsletter)
loc: UK-based (editor in London), covers US AI policy primarily
notes_head: Founded May 2024 by Shakeel Hashim, former Economist journalist. Weekly newsletter covering AI policy and safety. Funded by Tarbell Fellowship (supported by Open Philanthropy). Grew from 1-person to 3-person newsroom by 2025. Key team includes Managing Editor Jasper Jackson, AI Reporter Celia Ford, and Opinion Editor Jamie Condliffe. Cited by major outlets including NYT, Guardian, Politico. Focuses on AI governance, frontier lab developments, and policy analysis for decision-makers.

name: TIME Magazine
notes_head: Founded 1923. Launched TIME AI Agent in November 2025 - an AI-powered platform built with Scale AI for interactive journalism. Key AI reporters include Andrew R. Chow, Billy Perrigo, Harry Booth, and Tharin Pillay. Named 'Architects of AI' as 2025 Person of the Year. Has licensing agreement with OpenAI for archive access. Major cover stories include 'The People vs. AI' (Feb 2026) and extensive Anthropic coverage.

name: Explainable
notes_head: Media organization focused on translating AI developments into accessible stories for public understanding. Mission is 'empowering organizations, researchers, and creators to translate the future of AI into stories the world understands.' Very limited public information available about team size, funding, or specific outputs. Appears to be primarily a storytelling/communication platform rather than a technical research organization.

name: Manifold Markets
notes_head: Founded 2021 by James Grugett and others. Raised $3M total funding including from Alameda Research. Play-money prediction market platform with 100,000+ markets covering politics, tech, AI timelines, and regulation. Uses 'mana' currency that can be donated to charity. Academic studies show play-money markets achieve accuracy within 3-5 percentage points of real-money platforms. Popular among EA/rationalist communities for forecasting practice.

name: Donations List Website
notes_head: Created 2017 by mathematician Vipul Naik with contributions from Issa Rice. Tracks $407M+ in AI safety donations (2007-2023) from major funders like Open Philanthropy ($276M), MIRI ($44M), OpenAI. Open-source database covers 875 donations across 191 organizations. Data considered preliminary until July 2026 completion target. Serves EA/rationalist communities primarily.

name: An Overview of the AI Safety Funding Situation
notes_head: Comprehensive analysis published on EA Forum mapping AI safety funding ecosystem from 2022-2025. Tracks $3.9B in total funding with Safe Superintelligence raising $3B across two rounds. Documents shift from philanthropic to venture funding in AI safety space. Referenced extensively by other funders and researchers as authoritative overview of funding landscape.

name: ML & AI Safety Updates
notes_head: Weekly podcast, YouTube channel and newsletter that provided updates on AI safety research and developments. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map, discontinued due to organizational reprioritization. Was featured on the Alignment Forum and served as a media source for the AI safety community to stay informed about latest research and policy developments.

name: Time (AI coverage)
notes_head: Time magazine has extensively covered AI developments, with correspondents Andrew R. Chow and Billy Perrigo leading AI coverage through their newsletter 'In the Loop'. The publication has launched TIME AI initiatives including the TIME AI Agent, developed in collaboration with Scale AI, which provides personalized, interactive journalism experiences. Time has also partnered with OpenAI to advance global AI literacy and created AI-powered features like daily audio briefs and translation services. The magazine named 'The Architects of AI' as the 2025 Person of the Year, reflecting AI's significa

name: Reddit
notes_head: Reddit has lucrative AI data licensing deals with Google ($60M annually) and OpenAI ($70M annually), representing ~10% of $1.3B revenue. The platform launched Reddit Answers AI search tool powered by Google Gemini in late 2024, growing from 1M to 15M weekly users by Q4 2025. CEO Steve Huffman aims to make Reddit a 'go-to search engine' while implementing policies to label AI-generated content and preserve human authenticity. The platform blocked most automated crawlers in 2024 and sued Anthropic for unauthorized scraping.

name: Asteria Film Co.
notes_head: Asteria Film Co. is actually an AI-powered film production studio, not a traditional media/journalism organization. Founded by Bryn Mooser and Natasha Lyonne, it focuses on creating 'ethical AI' models for film production while developing the Continuum Suite operating system for streamlined filmmaking workflows. Backed by CAA and Comcast Ventures.

name: Exponential View
notes_head: Founded and written by Azeem Azhar, prominent AI policy commentator and author of 'Exponential'. Weekly newsletter with 200,000+ subscribers analyzing AI's economic and societal impacts. Co-authored research with Epoch AI on AI economics and regularly interviews tech leaders and policymakers.

name: Wired
notes_head: Major technology publication with dedicated AI coverage team including senior writers Will Knight, Maxwell Zeff, and Zeyi Yang. Academic research shows WIRED's AI coverage from 2018-2023 had predominantly positive sentiment with increasing polarization over time. Reaches over 30 million people monthly and is considered one of the most influential technology publications globally.

name: MIT Technology Review
notes_head: Leading technology publication with dedicated AI reporting team including Will Douglas Heaven (senior AI editor), Michelle Kim, and others. Coverage spans technical developments, policy analysis, and societal impacts. Known for annual '10 Breakthrough Technologies' list and deep investigative pieces on AI industry practices.

name: Bloomberg
notes_head: Bloomberg has extensive AI coverage across multiple verticals including technology, markets, and legal analysis. Operates dedicated AI section with regular reporting on policy developments, market trends, and corporate adoption. Bloomberg Law provides specialized AI legal analysis. The company has also developed its own AI products including ASKB for the Bloomberg Terminal, built on proprietary AI capabilities dating back to 2009.

name: Financial Times
notes_head: Has dedicated AI editor Madhumita Murgia (appointed 2023) and comprehensive AI coverage hub. Uses AI internally for story discovery through automated data pipelines and computational journalism. Partners with MIT Technology Review on 'The State of AI' newsletter series covering geopolitical AI implications.

name: Associated Press
notes_head: AP was a pioneer in establishing AI standards in journalism contracts in 2023. Produces 5,000 pieces of content daily with 230 locations globally. Has licensing deals with OpenAI (2023) and Google (2025) for content use in AI systems. Operates extensive AI initiatives including Local Lede federal data monitoring, video analysis tools, and automated transcription systems.

name: The Washington Post
notes_head: Launched AI & Tech Brief newsletter in 2026 covering AI as economic and regulatory transformation. Deployed controversial 'Your Personal Podcast' AI tool in December 2025 that generated fabricated quotes and faced internal newsroom backlash. Also offers 'Ask The Post AI' search tool and AI-generated audio articles. Partnership with ElevenLabs for voice technology and Virginia Tech for AI development.

name: Vox Media
notes_head: Has comprehensive AI coverage across multiple verticals including Future Perfect section. Signed licensing deal with OpenAI in 2024. Union contract includes AI protections preventing layoffs solely from AI implementation. Staff includes reporters like Eric Levitz, Bryan Walsh, and Sigal Samuel covering AI policy and implications extensively.

name: New York Times
notes_head: The New York Times is a media organization dedicated to seeking the truth and helping people understand the world through independent journalism. The company has over 11.4 million subscribers and employs approximately 2,000 journalists in its newsroom. The New York Times has created an A.I. Initiatives team led by Editorial Director Zach Seward to leverage artificial intelligence for journalism while maintaining human oversight. The organization filed a lawsuit against Perplexity AI for unauthorized use of Times content.

name: Platformer
notes_head: Independent tech newsletter founded in 2020 by Casey Newton, focusing on the intersection of technology and democracy. Covers major AI labs and platforms with a critical, product-focused lens. Subscriber-funded via Ghost; Newton declined Substack advances and moved the publication off Substack in 2024 over content moderation concerns.

name: Wall Street Journal
notes_head: Major U.S. business newspaper owned by News Corp (Rupert Murdoch). Covers AI extensively through its tech and business desks and has employed prominent AI journalists including Karen Hao (China tech, 2022–2023). Has adopted AI-generated tools internally (Narrativa for market summaries, AI article summaries) and its editor-in-chief Emma Tucker has been openly pro-AI in newsrooms. Editorial coverage is business-focused and generally frames AI as an economic and competitive opportunity.

name: The American Scholar
notes_head: Quarterly magazine of public affairs, literature, science, history, and culture published by Phi Beta Kappa Society since 1932. Named after Ralph Waldo Emerson's 1837 oration. Multiple National Magazine Award winner. Publishes essays, criticism, poetry, and (since 2006) fiction. Also maintains daily website content and bimonthly e-newsletter. Relevant to AI discourse through long-form cultural and intellectual essays on technology and society.

name: Quartz
notes_head: Digitally native business news outlet founded in 2012 by Atlantic Media, now owned by Redbrick (Canadian software firm). Organized around 'obsessions' rather than traditional beats. Notable for AI integration controversy: in January 2025, drew significant attention for quietly publishing AI-generated news articles — described as 'purely experimental' AI newsroom intended to free editorial staff for deeper reporting. Publishes 'Quartz AI & Tech' newsletter covering business of AI and technology. Uses in-house Chartbuilder tool and Atlas data visualization platform. Previously employed Karen Hao

name: Pulitzer Center
notes_head: Nonprofit journalism organization (Pulitzer Center on Crisis Reporting) supporting independent journalists on underreported global issues. Major AI accountability journalism hub: runs AI Accountability Network with dedicated fellowships, reporting grants, ML reporting grants, and AI Spotlight Series (2,000+ journalists trained since 2024). AI Accountability Fellowship launched 2022, supporting 27 journalists from five continents. Awards $2M+ annually in direct grants. Stories by grantees have been cited by courts, sparked official investigations. Funded by MacArthur Foundation, Omidyar Network

name: Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism
notes_head: Public graduate journalism school at CUNY (est. 2006), the only public graduate journalism school in the northeastern U.S. Named after Craig Newmark (craigslist founder) who donated $20M (2018) and an additional $10M endowment for ethics/security center. AI relevance: runs AI Journalism Labs (5th and 6th cohorts in 2026 with Microsoft support and Nordic AI Journalism partnership) training experienced journalists on generative AI tools. Community Media Training program helps niche outlets report on how AI systems affect immigrants, communities of color, and vulnerable populations. Working towar

name: Canadian Journalism Foundation
notes_head: Canadian non-profit organization founded in 1990 that promotes, celebrates, and facilitates excellence in journalism. Runs annual journalism awards and fosters education, training, and research opportunities. Partner of the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Launched the CJF-Hinton Award for Excellence in AI Safety Reporting in October 2025, named for Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton, recognizing journalism that critically examines AI safety implications. Conducts media literacy research on AI-generated misinformation — a 2023 study found 58%% of Canadians believed they had encountered AI-generated mi

name: Reuters
notes_head: Reuters is an international news agency owned by Thomson Reuters, one of the two largest wire services globally. Its U.S. technology desk has expanded AI coverage significantly since 2023, with dedicated AI reporters covering frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic), governance, and enterprise AI adoption. Widely syndicated — Reuters wire reporting often sets the initial framing of AI-industry stories in regional and international outlets.

name: Semafor
notes_head: Semafor is a global news organization founded in 2022 by Justin Smith and Ben Smith. Its Technology vertical, led by Technology Editor Reed Albergotti, publishes a twice-weekly AI-focused newsletter read by an estimated 30,000+ tech executives. Also produces Semafor Flagship (daily newsletter) and hosts the annual Semafor AI Summit in Washington, D.C., which convenes AI-industry executives and policymakers.

name: Forbes
notes_head: Forbes is a U.S. business magazine and media company founded in 1917. Its AI coverage is anchored by the annual AI 50 list (most promising private AI companies, produced with Sequoia and Meritech) and a growing dedicated AI reporting team. Hired Anna Tong from Reuters in October 2025 to lead AI coverage and help curate the AI 50.

name: Alex Bores campaign
notes_head: New York State Assembly Member (District 73) running for Congress in NY-12. Former Palantir engineer who left in 2019 citing opposition to ICE contracts. Authored RAISE Act requiring AI companies with $500M+ revenue to publish safety plans. Named by TIME as one of 100 most influential people in AI. Campaign has raised $234,681 as of 2026. Target of $10M+ spending by industry-backed super PAC Leading the Future opposing his pro-regulation stance.

name: Leading the Future
funding_model: Super PAC with over $100 million in initial funding from AI industry figures including Andreessen Horowitz, Greg Brockman, Anna Brockman, Ron Conway, Joe Lonsdale, and Perplexity. Raised $125 million in late 2025 with $70 million cash on hand entering 2026.
notes_head:
Leading the Future (FEC ID: C00916114) is a bipartisan super PAC launched by a coalition of AI companies and industry leaders with the mission to ensure U.S. leadership in AI innovation and development. It engages in independent expenditures to support candidates aligned with pro-AI agendas, legislative advocacy, and grassroots efforts across federal and state levels, with operations beginning in 2025 and plans to expand nationwide in 2026.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916114). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live sp

name: Think Big
funding_model: Political/PAC (sub-PAC of Leading the Future; funded by a16z/Brockman/Lonsdale/Conway)
notes_head: Democratic-aligned super PAC within the Leading the Future network (pro-AI industry). Sister PAC to Republican-focused American Mission. Funded primarily by Leading the Future ($10M) plus Ron Conway ($500K) and Joe Lonsdale ($250K); raised ~$10.5M total by March 2026. Opposes candidates who support AI regulation; spent ~$2.42M against Alex Bores in NY-12.

name: American Mission
funding_model: Super PAC funded primarily by Leading the Future ($10 million), Lonsdale Enterprises Inc. ($250,000), Greg Brockman/OpenAI ($25 million), and Andreessen Horowitz ($25 million). Raised approximately $10.25 million total as of early 2026.
notes_head:
Supported candidates in TX and NC races. Backed Chris Gober, Tom Sell, Jessica Steinmann, Laurie Buckhout, Clay Fuller, Jace Yarbrough.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916692). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.

name: Defending Our Values PAC
funding_model: Political/PAC (funded through Public First Action; Anthropic primary donor)
notes_head: Republican-aligned super PAC affiliated with Public First Action (backed by Anthropic's $20M donation). Supports GOP candidates who favor AI safety regulation and state-level AI oversight. FEC ID C00928390. Backed Alexandra Mealer, Carlos De La Cruz, and Pete Ricketts. Counterpart to Democrats-focused Jobs and Democracy PAC.

name: Jobs and Democracy PAC
funding_model: Political/PAC (funded through Public First Action; Anthropic primary donor)
notes_head: Democratic-aligned super PAC affiliated with Public First Action, funded by Anthropic's $20M donation. Backs Democratic candidates who support AI safety regulation and state-level AI oversight. FEC ID C00928374. Major spends for Valerie Foushee (~$1.61M), Alex Bores (~$468K), and Colin Allred (~$150K).

name: Dream NYC
funding_model: Super PAC funded by Daniel Ziegler of Anthropic PBC ($50,000 raised as of Dec 31, 2025)
notes_head:
Supported Alex Bores ($1,332).

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00928069). Classified as a pro-safety AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.

name: Republican Party
notes_head: The Republican Party is confronting an unfamiliar fracture over artificial intelligence, with the battleground being AI regulation and the combatants being red-state lawmakers who want to protect their constituents versus a White House determined to clear the runway for Big Tech. Governors like Ron DeSantis and Spencer Cox are championing state-level AI regulations, focusing on a consumer 'Bill of Rights' and protecting children. The Trump administration has flipped the script, aggressively pressuring Republican state legislators to abandon bills designed to protect children and establish basi

name: Democratic Party
notes_head: The Democratic Party is the major liberal political party in the United States, sitting on the center to center-left of the political spectrum, founded in 1828, making it the world's oldest active political party. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries announced the launch of a new House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy that will convene throughout 2026. The Democratic National Committee (DNC) has been the heart of the Democratic Party since 1848, and is the oldest continuing political party in the United States. Ken Martin serves as the current Chairperson of the Democrat

name: National Republican Senatorial Committee
notes_head: The National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) is the Republican Hill committee for the United States Senate, working to elect Republicans to the Senate. The NRSC was founded in 1916 as the Republican Senatorial Campaign Committee and was reorganized in 1948 and renamed the National Republican Senatorial Committee. It has been chaired by Senator Tim Scott from South Carolina since 2025. The NRSC raised $88 million in 2025, which is the best off-year fundraising for the Committee since 2021.

name: Club for Growth Action
notes_head: Conservative super PAC affiliated with Club for Growth. Supports limited-government, free-market candidates. Major donors include Peter Thiel and Richard Uihlein. Focuses on tax cuts, deregulation, free trade, school choice. No specific AI policy position — general deregulatory stance would extend to opposing AI regulation. Founded 2010 as independent expenditure arm.

name: Public First Action
notes_head: Bipartisan 501(c)(4) nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy, co-founded in 2025 by former Congressmen Brad Carson (D) and Chris Stewart (R). Received $20M from Anthropic in Feb 2026 — its largest single donation — to counter 'Leading the Future,' a $125M super PAC backed by tech billionaires opposing AI regulation. Policy priorities: AI model transparency safeguards, federal AI governance framework, smart export controls on AI chips, targeted regulation of near-term high risks (bioweapons, cyberattacks). Plans to back 30-50 candidates from both parties in 2026 state and federal races.

name: Brookings Institution
notes_head: The Brookings Institution is an American think tank in Washington, D.C. that conducts research and education in the social sciences, primarily in economics and tax policy, metropolitan policy, governance, foreign policy, global economy, and economic development. The organization was founded in 1916 as the Institute for Government Research (IGR), with the mission of becoming "the first private organization devoted to analyzing public policy issues at the national level". Brookings states that its staff "represent diverse points of view" and describes itself as nonpartisan. The institution is cu

name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
loc: Washington, DC (Georgetown University Walsh School of Foreign Service)
