notes_head: The Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET) is a policy research organization within Georgetown University's Walsh School of Foreign Service that was founded in 2019. When founded, it was Washington's first think tank focused entirely on security and emerging technology, and it quickly grew to be the largest research organization in the world working on AI policy. CSET provides decision-makers with data-driven analysis on the security implications of emerging technologies, currently focusing on the effects of progress in artificial intelligence (AI), advanced computing and biotechno

name: RAND Corporation
loc: Santa Monica, CA (with offices in Arlington VA, Pittsburgh PA, Cambridge UK, Brussels, Canberra)
notes_head: RAND Corporation is a nonprofit, nonpartisan research organization that provides leaders with the information they need to make evidence-based decisions. The organization studies the potential opportunities and risks of artificial intelligence, including ways to strengthen the AI workforce, countering AI-driven social media manipulation by U.S. adversaries, AI governance and how to make future AI systems safer, potential military applications of AI, how AI is being used in the classroom, and identifying occupations at risk of being replaced by AI. RAND operates the RAND Center on AI, Security,

name: Forecasting Research Institute
loc: Philadelphia, PA with distributed team across US, UK, and other countries
notes_head: The Forecasting Research Institute develops forecasting methods to improve decision-making on high-stakes issues. The organization employs 41 people and operates as a 501(c)(3) research nonprofit. FRI was founded in 2021 with initial support from Coefficient Giving. The institute's flagship project is the Existential Risk Persuasion Tournament (XPT), which brought together 169 participants who made forecasts about existential threats including AI, biosecurity, climate change, and nuclear war.

name: Effective Institutions Project
loc: San Francisco Bay Area (operates globally with staff in US, UK, Taiwan, South Africa, Norway, Belgium, Canada)
notes_head: The Effective Institutions Project is a philanthropic advisory and research organization that seeks out and incubates high-impact strategies to improve institutional decision-making around the world. AI governance has been a core focus for the organization since its founding in 2021, and the majority of the organization's resources are currently devoted toward making progress on this important and timely issue. The organization has 12 employees according to LinkedIn data. EIP works in partnership with funders and civil society organizations across the globe to coordinate efforts, share knowled

name: AI Standards Lab
loc: Remote/distributed (team across US, Spain, Canada, Lithuania, Netherlands, UK, Germany)
notes_head: AI Standards Lab is a remote organization that aims to accelerate AI safety standards writing in organizations like ISO/IEC, CEN-CENELEC, the IEEE, and NIST. The organization employs 10 people and is structured as a virtual lab bringing together volunteers from around the world. AI Standards Lab has actively participated in the EU AI Act's General-Purpose AI Code of Practice drafting process, submitting detailed technical feedback across multiple drafts. The organization has published extensive reports analyzing EU AI Act amendments and providing recommendations on various regulatory proposals

name: SaferAI
loc: Paris, France (with staff across Belgium, UK, Spain, UAE)
notes_head: SaferAI is a French non-profit furthering risk management in policy and industry. SaferAI works across research and policy to make AI safer by advancing responsible AI development through modeling complex risks, improving company practices, and contributing to standards and policies. SaferAI has been selected by the European Commission's AI Office to provide technical assistance on assessing risks posed by GPAI models at EU level, winning Lot 1 of the 'Technical Assistance for AI Safety' tender. SaferAI released its first ratings evaluating the risk-management practices of top AI companies, wi

name: AI Now Institute
notes_head: The AI Now Institute was founded in 2017 as a research institute studying the social implications of artificial intelligence and policy research that addresses the concentration of power in the tech industry. As of mid-2022, AI Now is operating as an independent organization and does not accept funding from corporate donors, including the tech industry we aim to hold accountable. The institute's co-executive directors are Amba Kak and Sarah Myers West. AI Now's leadership were invited to advise the US Federal Trade Commission on artificial intelligence in 2021.

name: Foresight Institute
loc: San Francisco, CA (HQ at 50 California Street, Suite 1500)
notes_head: Foresight Institute is a research organization founded in 1986 by Christine Peterson and Eric Drexler. The organization works to advance science and technology for the benefit of life through grants, events, prizes, and fellowships. Foresight Institute is led by CEO Allison Duettmann and COO Karolina Eklöw. The organization focuses on six areas including Secure AI, Neurotechnology, Longevity, Biotechnology, Nanotechnology, Space and Existential Hope.

name: Secure AI Project
notes_head: Secure AI Project is a Public Policy Offices company that employs 6 people and is classified as a nonprofit organization. The organization advocates for legally requiring large AI developers to publish safety and security protocols, protect whistleblowers who reveal unsafe practices, and provide clear incentives for developers to mitigate risk in accordance with industry best practices. Secure AI Project co-sponsored California's SB 53 bill, which was signed into law and establishes world-leading AI safety disclosure requirements. The organization has supported legislation in nine states inclu

name: New Consensus
notes_head: New Consensus is an agile think tank focused on designing comprehensive, detailed plans for governments to achieve economic renewal and transformation. The organization was founded in 2017 and employs 3 people, with a -60.0% year-over-year change. New Consensus is the author of the Green New Deal and Mission for America. The organization's guiding principle states that "It is possible to create a world where the people of every nation and community control the means to sustainably make a prosperous living".

name: Center for a New American Security (CNAS)
notes_head: The Center for a New American Security (CNAS) is an independent, bipartisan, nonprofit organization that develops strong, pragmatic, and principled national security and defense policies. CNAS was established in 2007 by co-founders Dr. Kurt M. Campbell and Michèle A. Flournoy. The organization is located in Washington and engages policymakers, experts, and the public with innovative, fact-based research, ideas, and analysis to shape and elevate the national security debate. CNAS has strong ties to the Democratic Party and was formerly led by CEO Victoria Nuland, who served as undersecretary of

name: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
notes_head: The Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI) is building a global research community dedicated to helping humanity navigate the transition to a world with advanced AI. GovAI was founded at Yale University in 2016, with Prof. Allan Dafoe as its Founding Director, became an academic center at the University of Oxford in 2018 and then spun out to become a non-profit in 2021. GovAI is now led by Ben Garfinkel. The organization draws on political science, computer science, economics, law, and philosophy in its research.

name: AI Policy Institute
notes_head: The AI Policy Institute (AIPI) is a research and advocacy non-profit dedicated to seeking political solutions to potential catastrophic risks from emerging AI technology. The organization was founded by Daniel Colson to advocate for ethical oversight of AI for mitigating potential catastrophic risks posed by AI. AIPI's mission is to channel public concern about AI into effective governance by engaging with policymakers, media, and the public to shape a future where AI is developed responsibly and transparently. The organization conducts regular polling of US voters to assess public concern abo

name: Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI)
notes_head: Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to policy advocacy in the public interest, focused on emerging technologies like artificial intelligence (AI). ARI was launched on March 6, 2024, and is headquartered in Washington, D.C.. The organization has a sister organization called Center for Responsible Innovation (CRI), which is a 501(c)(3) organization that focuses on issue promotion, policy research and development, and educational programming, while ARI is a 501(c)(4) organization that focuses on lobbying, advocacy, and political activities. ARI is fund

name: Future of Life Institute
loc: Campbell, CA (with offices in DC, London, Brussels)
notes_head: The Future of Life Institute (FLI) is a nonprofit organization founded in March 2014 that aims to steer transformative technology towards benefiting life and away from large-scale risks, with a focus on existential risk from advanced artificial intelligence. FLI's stated mission is to steer transformative technology towards benefiting life and away from extreme large-scale risks. The organization engages in grantmaking, policy research and advocacy, and an array of education and outreach projects. FLI is officially registered in Pennsylvania, with offices in Campbell, US, and Brussels, Belgium

name: Centre for Long-Term Resilience
notes_head: The Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR) is an independent think tank with a mission to transform global resilience to extreme risks. CLTR was set up five years ago with a vision for a safe and flourishing world with high resilience to extreme risks. They are based in the heart of Whitehall and are independent and non-partisan, ensuring that Government receives genuinely impartial thinking. Their core focus areas are AI risk, biological risk and government risk management.

name: Center for American Progress
notes_head: The Center for American Progress is an independent, nonpartisan policy institute that is dedicated to improving the lives of all Americans through bold, progressive ideas, as well as strong leadership and concerted action. The Center for American Progress was created in 2003 as a Democratic alternative to conservative think tanks such as The Heritage Foundation and the American Enterprise Institute. As of 2025, CAP's board of directors includes Democratic politician Julian Castro, Sandler Foundation president Steve Daetz, businessman Andrew Hauptman, former Bain Capital partner Kristin Mugford

name: Berggruen Institute
loc: Los Angeles, CA (with centers in Beijing, Venice)
notes_head: The Berggruen Institute's mission is to develop foundational ideas and shape political, economic, and social institutions for the 21st century. The Institute brings together some of the best minds and most authoritative voices from across cultural and political boundaries to explore fundamental questions of our time. Founded in 2010, the Berggruen Institute was established by Nicolas Berggruen and Nathan Gardels after contemplating economic and political stresses caused by the global financial crisis. The Institute has centers in Los Angeles, Beijing (at Peking University), and Venice, Italy,

name: Transformative Futures Institute
loc: United States (works with policymakers in DC)
notes_head: 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization. Executive Director Ross Gruetzemacher is Assistant Professor at Wichita State University and Research Affiliate at Cambridge's Centre for the Study of Existential Risk. Specializes in underutilized foresight techniques including wargaming (Intelligence Rising simulation game), horizon scanning, and agent-based modeling. Disseminates research directly to DC policymakers and through academic publishing. Co-signed congressional letter supporting NIST AI Safety Institute funding in 2024.

name: AI Governance & Safety Canada
notes_head: Founded 2022, nonpartisan nonprofit with 15 employees (+50% YoY growth). Executive Director Wyatt Tessari L'Allié has testified before Canadian parliamentary committees (ETHI, SRSR) on AI regulation. Key publication: 'Preparing for the AI Crisis: A Plan for Canada' (October 2025) white paper. Board member Evan R. Murphy also serves at UC Berkeley's AI Security Initiative. Produces policy recommendations, legislative submissions, and government briefings.

name: Center for Law & AI Risk
loc: University of Alabama (Arbel) + University of Houston (Salib)
notes_head: Academic research center co-directed by Yonathan Arbel (University of Alabama) and Peter Salib (University of Houston). Small organization with ~2 people. Publishes legal scholarship on AI safety law, including pioneering work on AI agent identification and liability ('How to Count AIs' paper). Hosts academic retreats and workshops. Focuses on building field of legal scholars working on catastrophic AI risk reduction through law.

name: Centre for Future Generations
notes_head: Founded 2023 by tech entrepreneur Steven Schuurman and social entrepreneur Laurens de Groot. Staff of 27 people with 41.9% YoY growth. Entirely funded by philanthropy including Dreamery Foundation, Tanka Foundation, and Powoki Foundation. Key publications include 'Advanced AI: Possible Futures' scenario analysis, EU AI Act policy recommendations, and 'Strategic Innovation for European Security' project. Provides policy submissions to European Commission on AI governance and digital strategy.

name: Good Ancestors
notes_head: Founded 2022 as Australian charity dedicated to long-term future of humanity. Small team led by CEO Greg Sadler (15 years public service experience). Funded by individual donors, US/European foundations, and government research contracts. Key outputs include AI Legislation Stress Test (2025) engaging 64 experts, Australia AI Policy White Paper 2025-2028, and parliamentary submissions. Coordinates Australians for AI Safety coalition. Testified before Senate Select Committee on AI.

name: Future Matters
notes_head: Founded 2020. German nonprofit (HRB 251407 B) with U.S. 501(c)(3) equivalency. Think tank and capacity-building hub working on climate protection, AI governance, and biosecurity. Coordinated messaging for 20 AI safety organizations at UK AI Safety Summit 2023. Provides strategy consulting, policy prioritization research, and training services. Client rating of 8.4/10 for AI governance consulting services.

name: The AI Policy Network
notes_head: Founded as 501(c)(4) advocacy organization led by Executive Director Daniel Colson (also founder of AI Policy Institute). Team includes former Pentagon AI Policy Director Mark Beall Jr., former congressional staff from Pelosi and Schumer offices. Submitted detailed policy recommendations to Trump administration's AI RFI in March 2025. Conducts polling research showing voter support for AI regulation in swing districts. Sister organization AI Policy Institute conducts survey research.

name: Rethink Priorities
loc: San Francisco, CA (team distributed across US, UK, and other countries)
notes_head: Founded 2018 by Marcus Davis and Peter Wildeford. Cross-cause research organization with ~46 employees covering AI safety, animal welfare, global health, and longtermist priorities. 2026 budget of $7.5M core/$9.3M expanded. Major funders include Open Philanthropy, Good Ventures Foundation, Silicon Valley Community Foundation. Special Projects program fiscally sponsors AI safety organizations like Apollo Research, Epoch, and Institute for AI Policy & Strategy. Launching dedicated AI Strategy team in 2026.

name: Odyssean Institute
loc: United Kingdom (with global team across 4 continents)
notes_head: Founded 2022 by Giuseppe Dal Pra. Team of 12 researchers across 4 continents. Develops the 'Odyssean Process' combining expert horizon scanning, decision-making under deep uncertainty (DMDU), and citizen assemblies. Key projects include Global Catastrophic Risk horizon scan (2024), GRAIN resilience network, and planned AI governance citizen assembly for 2026. Collaborates with ALLFED on resilience research.

name: ILINA Program
loc: Nairobi, Kenya (with presence in Nigeria, Zimbabwe, Mauritius)
notes_head: Founded 2022, African-led research program with 14 employees. Coordinates Cecil Abungu, runs Junior Research Fellowship program for talent development. Publishes research papers and policy briefs on AI governance from Global South perspectives. Recent work includes 'Assessing the Case for Africa-Centric AI Safety Evaluations' and analysis of AU Continental AI Strategy. Hosts policy engagement sessions and collaborates with international AI safety community.

name: Public AI Network
loc: Global network, coordination hub appears US-based
notes_head: Founded 2023 as global movement and network promoting AI as public infrastructure. Maintains extensive publication database with 150+ papers, policy briefs, and research from multiple organizations. Key publications include Public AI Whitepaper (2024), policy briefs on European digital sovereignty, and proposals for public AI utilities. Affiliated researchers from institutions like Harvard, Vanderbilt, Mozilla, and various European policy organizations. Operates inference utility providing access to open-source AI models via HuggingFace.

name: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)
notes_head: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) is a bipartisan Washington, D.C.-based think tank founded in 1962. One of the leading U.S. foreign-policy and national-security research institutions. On AI, CSIS houses the Wadhwani AI Center (launched 2024, directed by Gregory C. Allen) which publishes research on AI and national security, chip export controls, and U.S.-China technology competition, and produces the biweekly CSIS AI Policy Podcast.

name: Association for Long Term Existence and Resilience (ALTER)
notes_head: Founded 2022, Israeli nonprofit with 5 staff. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Led by David Manheim. Institutional member of US AI Safety Consortium (NIST). Key work includes AI oversight standards, biosecurity policy, and salt iodization advocacy. Publishes research on AI governance gaps and incident preparedness frameworks.

name: 80,000 Hours
loc: Oxford, UK + Berkeley, CA + NYC offices
notes_head: Founded 2011 by William MacAskill and Ben Todd. Career advisory nonprofit with 46 employees focusing on directing talent toward high-impact careers, especially AI safety/governance. Published extensive problem profiles on AI risks, operates job board, provides 1-on-1 career advice. Over 3,000 people report changing careers due to their guidance. Primary funder: Coefficient Giving. Part of effective altruism community.

name: Beijing Institute of AI Safety and Governance (Beijing-AISI)
notes_head: Founded September 2024 with Beijing municipal government support. Led by Yi Zeng, CAS professor and UN High-Level Advisory Body on AI member. Core contributor to China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA). Published AGILE Index 2025 evaluating AI governance across 40 countries. Co-organized Beijing AI Safety International Consensus with signatures from Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, Stuart Russell.

name: China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA)
loc: China (multi-institutional network based primarily in Beijing)
notes_head: Launched February 2025 at Paris AI Action Summit. Network of China's leading AI institutions including Tsinghua University, Beijing Academy of AI, Shanghai AI Lab, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and China Academy of Information and Communications Technology. Claims Chinese government backing but operates as collaborative platform rather than regulatory body. Primary function is international representation and dialogue coordination rather than domestic oversight or testing.

name: Talos Fellowship (Talos Network)
notes_head: Founded 2022 as EU Tech Policy Fellowship, renamed to Talos Network. Flagship 7-month program trains ~50 fellows annually in European AI governance through 8-week online curriculum, Brussels summit, and 4-6 month paid placements. 70% alumni transition rate to AI policy roles across EU AI Office, UK AISI, OECD, think tanks. Funded primarily by Coefficient Giving/Good Ventures, seeking €200k diversification funding in 2026. Managing Director: David Conrad.

name: Metaculus
notes_head: Founded 2015 as public benefit corporation. Staff of 30. Operates forecasting platform with pro forecasters and AI policy workshops. Partnership with Future of Life Institute on AI Pathways project supporting U.S. policymakers. Open Philanthropy commissioned AI progress tournament. Board member Peter Wildeford co-founded Institute for AI Policy and Strategy.

name: Simon Institute for Longterm Governance
notes_head: Founded 2021, nonprofit think tank with 5 staff based in Geneva. Funded by Open Philanthropy and other EA-aligned donors. Specializes in international AI governance and multilateral diplomacy. Key work includes designing frameworks for UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI, organizing briefings for diplomats, and publishing policy recommendations for international AI summits. Named after Herbert Simon, focusing on future-oriented policymaking tools.

name: The Future Society
loc: Boston, MA (with Brussels office and global team across 12 countries)
notes_head: Founded 2014, nonprofit with 29 employees (72% growth YoY). Funded by Patrick J. McGovern Foundation ($170K grant 2023). Key activities include organizing The Athens Roundtable on AI and the Rule of Law, participating in EU AI Act Code of Practice development, conducting global consultations for France's 2025 AI Action Summit involving 10,000+ citizens and 200+ experts. Notable reports include comprehensive analysis of AI agents governance under EU AI Act and AI red lines governance framework.

name: Vista Institute for AI Policy
loc: Washington, DC (with staff in US, Kenya, UK)
notes_head: Founded 2024 as fiscally sponsored project of Rethink Priorities (EIN 84-3896318). Staff of 6 people with 60% YoY growth. Offers virtual courses on National Security Law and AI, AI Agents and the Law, plus in-person DC workshops. Trained 100+ law and policy students, researchers, and experts in 2025. Provides fellowships for independent research and research assistant positions. Co-Director Aishwarya Saxena acknowledged in academic papers on AI governance.

name: Convergence Analysis
notes_head: Founded 2017 by David Kristoffersson and Justin Shovelain, formalized as institute in 2024. Non-profit with 9-person interdisciplinary team spanning AI alignment, ethics, governance, and policy. Funded by Future of Life Institute, Open Philanthropy, and individual donors. Key programs: AI Clarity (scenario research), Governance Recommendations, and AI Awareness. Published major reports including '2024 State of AI Regulatory Landscape' and 'Analysis of Global AI Governance Strategies'. Hosted Threshold 2030 conference on AI economics. Research has influenced US BIS dual-use AI reporting require

name: Institute for Law & AI (LawAI)
loc: Boston, MA (with offices in UK, Germany, Denmark, Canada, Australia)
notes_head: Founded as independent think tank, formerly Legal Priorities Project. Staff of 40+ researchers across 12 countries, led by Founding Director Christoph Winter. Publishes working paper series via SSRN, produces policy reports and research articles on AI governance. Notable recent work includes 'Legal Alignment for Safe and Ethical AI' and 'Automated Compliance and the Regulation of AI'. Hosts workshops on law-following AI and maintains research collaborations with academic institutions.

name: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) AI Policy Fellowship
notes_head: Nonpartisan think tank producing policy research on AI implications. Fellowship program runs 3 months with $15,000-$22,000 stipends, training professionals from varied backgrounds in AI policy. Alumni have secured positions at RAND, Institute for Progress, Center for Health Security, and government agencies. Recent research includes strategic visions in AI governance and policy recommendations for the AI Action Plan. Features mentorship from leading experts and produces concrete policy outputs like briefings to government officials and international conferences.

name: Association for Long Term Existence and Resilience
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Israeli research and advocacy nonprofit working to investigate, demonstrate, and foster useful ways to safeguard and improve the future of humanity.

name: Quantified Uncertainty Research Institute
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Advancing forecasting and epistemics to improve the long-term future of humanity. Conducts research and builds software.

name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Georgetown University think tank providing decision-makers with data-driven analysis on the security implications of emerging technologies.

name: The AI Whistleblower Initiative
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Helps AI insiders raise concerns about potential risks and misbehavior in AI development by providing whistleblowing services, expert guidance, and secure communication tools.

name: Public AI
notes_head: Public AI is a nonprofit initiative advocating for public-interest AI infrastructure as an alternative to purely commercial frontier AI development. Positions public AI as critical digital infrastructure (analogous to public broadcasting or libraries) and promotes government and civil-society funded AI projects, open models, and democratic oversight of large-scale AI systems.

name: Roosevelt Institute
notes_head: Progressive economic policy think tank with Nobel laureate Joseph Stiglitz as Chief Economist. AI work focuses on antitrust frameworks for emerging AI markets, worker rights in AI deployment, and democratic governance of automation. Argues labor voice must be central to technology policy; affiliated scholar Ganesh Sitaraman covers industrial policy.

name: Windfall Trust
notes_head: Windfall Trust is a research and advocacy project focused on the economic redistribution of gains from transformative AI, exploring mechanisms (equity stakes, dividends, public ownership) by which society could share in the windfall generated by advanced AI systems. Aligned with the post-labor-economics research community.

name: Collective Intelligence Project
notes_head: Nonprofit incubator for participatory AI governance models, co-founded by Divya Siddarth and Saffron Huang. Runs 'alignment assemblies' and community models to embed public input into AI development. Partnered with Anthropic, OpenAI, UK Frontier AI Task Force, and Taiwan's Digital Ministry. Obtained independent 501(c)3 status in 2024.

name: Plurality Institute
notes_head: Plurality Institute is a research and convening organization developing ''plural technology'' — tools and governance mechanisms that support diverse, decentralized collaboration. Associated with Glen Weyl''s research agenda (also linked to RadicalxChange) and engages AI policy from a democratic-infrastructure and pluralism lens rather than safety/alignment.

name: RadicalxChange
notes_head: RadicalxChange is a movement and nonprofit founded by economist Glen Weyl to advance mechanism-design innovations for democracy and markets — quadratic voting, quadratic funding, partial common ownership, and related tools. Active in AI policy discussions focused on collective decision-making, data dignity, and democratic governance of AI systems.

name: Civic Signals
notes_head: Research initiative co-founded by Eli Pariser and Talia Stroud, originally a project of the National Conference on Citizenship and the Center for Media Engagement at UT Austin. Developed a framework of 14 design principles ("signals") for evaluating and building healthier digital public spaces, based on a 20-country YouGov survey of 20,000+ people funded by the Omidyar Network. Rebranded as New_Public in 2021 and continues work on digital public infrastructure. Advocates for treating digital spaces like physical public infrastructure (libraries, parks, town squares) with civic design principle

name: Council on Foreign Relations
notes_head: The Council on Foreign Relations (CFR) is a nonpartisan, independent national membership organization, think tank, educator, and publisher founded in 1921. CFR publishes Foreign Affairs magazine and generates policy-relevant ideas and analysis on the most consequential issues facing the United States and the world. The organization has 1,277 employees and an annual revenue of $77.3 million. CFR's mission is to inform U.S. engagement with the world and convenes experts and policymakers to promote informed public discussion.

name: World Economic Forum
notes_head: The World Economic Forum is an independent, not-for-profit organization, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, with offices in New York and San Francisco, United States, Beijing, China, and Tokyo, Japan. Founded in 1971 as a not-for-profit foundation, the World Economic Forum set out with a transformative point of view: that business should not only to create economic value but also to serve society and the planet. The Forum is the International Organization for Public-Private Cooperation that engages the foremost political, business and other leaders of society to shape global, regional and i

name: Special Competitive Studies Project
notes_head: The Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP) is a bipartisan, non-profit initiative with a mission to make recommendations to strengthen America's long-term competitiveness as artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies reshape national security, economy, and society. SCSP carries forward the vital mission of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI), which was established in 2018. The organization was founded in 2021 and has 98 employees, located in Crystal City, United States. SCSP has launched multiple commissions and task forces, including colla

name: Colorado AI Policy Work Group
notes_head: Convened by Governor Jared Polis (Oct 2025) to revise Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205), the nation's first comprehensive AI anti-discrimination law. Diverse membership: consumer groups, hospitals, school districts, tech companies large and small. Delivered unanimous support for revised policy framework (March 2026) emphasizing transparency over bias audits: up-front notice when AI is used in consequential decisions, access to information on adverse decisions, right to correction, human review option. Recommendations to be codified in legislation.

name: Niskanen Center
notes_head: Centrist/center-right think tank founded 2015 by Jerry Taylor (ex-Cato). Named "Most Interesting Think Tank" by TIME (2023). AI policy stance: regulatory restraint, intervene only when harm is realistic. Has 501(c)(3) research arm and 501(c)(4) lobbying arm. Major funders include Hewlett Foundation, Open Philanthropy, Carnegie Corp.

name: Federation of American Scientists
notes_head: DC-based science and technology policy nonprofit founded in 1945. FAS runs the Day One Project to translate scientific expertise into federal policy; active in AI governance, advocating for responsible AI innovation, NIST AISI funding, and federal AI adoption. Submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House in 2025.

name: Munich Security Conference
notes_head: Premier annual international security conference held in Munich, Germany (since 1963). Increasingly prominent on AI governance. Convened 'Tech Accord to Combat Deceptive Use of AI in 2024 Elections' signed by 27 tech companies. Hosts Frontier AI Safety Commitments discussions. Technology program frames AI as 'decisive factor' in systemic competition. 2025 conference marked shift from AI safety to AI security framing.

name: Encode AI
notes_head: Youth-led DC-based nonprofit founded by Sneha Revanur advocating for responsible AI governance. Sponsored California SB 53 and New York's RAISE Act; challenged OpenAI's for-profit conversion. Funded by FLI, Heising-Simons, Survival and Flourishing Fund, and others; explicitly rejects corporate and AI-executive funding.

name: UNSG's AI Advisory Body
notes_head: UN Secretary-General's High-Level Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence, established October 2023. Comprised 39 preeminent AI leaders from 33 countries — geographically diverse, gender-balanced group from government, private sector, civil society, and academia. Published final report 'Governing AI for Humanity' in September 2024, proposing seven recommendations for global AI governance cooperation including foundations for the first globally inclusive and distributed architecture for AI governance. Process included 18 deep-dive discussions, 50+ consultation sessions, 50+ written submissions

name: British Standards Institute
notes_head: The British Standards Institution (BSI) is the UK's national standards body, playing a central role in shaping international AI governance standards. First certification body accredited by UKAS to certify ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System standard, published end of 2023) — also accredited by RvA (Netherlands) and ANAB (US). Actively involved in developing ISO/IEC 42001 and published ISO 42006 (July 2025) setting requirements for bodies auditing AI management systems. ISO/IEC 42001 addresses AI-specific aspects: unwanted bias, fairness, safety, security, privacy, accountability, explainabilit

name: ACM US Technology Policy Committee
notes_head: ACM's US Technology Policy Committee (USTPC), comprising 170+ members, serves as the focal point for ACM's interaction with all branches of the US government on technology policy. Has issued influential policy statements including 'Principles for Responsible Algorithmic Systems' (nine principles: legitimacy, harm minimization, transparency, accountability, etc.) and 'Principles for the Development, Deployment, and Use of Generative AI Technologies.' Called for immediate suspension of facial recognition in prejudicial circumstances. Recent outputs: Response to RFI on AI Regulatory Landscape (Oc

name: Bipartisan Policy Center
notes_head: Washington, D.C. think tank founded in 2007 by former Senate majority leaders Howard Baker, Tom Daschle, Bob Dole, and George Mitchell. 501(c)(3) nonprofit ($31.2M revenue FY2023). Runs an active AI policy program including an AI 101 education initiative for congressional staff, the AI and Energy Task Force, and policy research on governance frameworks. Advocates a use-case-specific, risk-based approach to AI accountability that balances innovation with civil rights, safety, and national security. Published 'Eight Considerations to Shape the Future of AI Governance.' CEO: Margaret Spellings (s

name: The Hastings Center
notes_head: Independent, nonpartisan bioethics research institute (est. 1969) based in Garrison, NY. Conducts major research program on Ethics of AI in Health and Biomedical Research. Received $800K five-year Donaghue Foundation award for research on trustworthy AI integration in healthcare. Launched 'Hastings on the Hill' initiative (Greenwall Foundation-funded) translating bioethics research on AI in health for policymakers. Created 'A Patient's Journey with Medical AI' interactive tool for policymakers, patients, and providers navigating medical AI ethics. President Vardit Ravitsky is PI on two NIH Bri

name: RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology
funding_model: Donor-supported independent research, directed government grants and contracts (DoD, DHS), philanthropic funding
notes_head: RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology (RAND CAST) is a research center within the RAND Corporation focused on transformative technologies and global security. Conducts policy research at the intersection of AI, cybersecurity, biotechnology, and national security. Research areas include AI and information security (securing frontier AI systems, assessing cyber capabilities), AI and biotechnology (preventing misuse, promoting responsible innovation), and building AI analysts for policy research at machine speed. Formed by merging the Technology and Security Policy Center and Meselson Cente

name: Forward Global
notes_head: International commercial and geopolitical risk advisory and public affairs firm headquartered in Paris, with ~470 staff and ~€100M in revenue. Offices in Paris, Brussels, London, Washington DC, New York, Los Angeles, and Miami, plus Africa. Specializes in public affairs, strategic communication, due diligence, and cybersecurity. In 2025, formed strategic partnership with Ballard Partners (leading US lobbying firm) for integrated lobbying and public affairs solutions worldwide. Partner of the Effective Institutions Project. While not an AI-focused think tank, its public affairs and regulatory a

name: Founders Pledge
notes_head: EA-aligned nonprofit where entrepreneurs pledge to donate a share of exit proceeds. Publishes cause-area research including advanced AI, recommending funders prioritize catastrophic-risk reduction and international coordination. Has moved over $323M in grants; funds organizations like FAR AI, CEIP, and the Effective Institutions Project.

name: Oxford Martin School
notes_head: Interdisciplinary research institute at the University of Oxford, founded 2005 via £70M+ endowment from James Martin. Houses the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative, which bridges technical AI research and global policy through research, fellowships, and engagement with the UN, EU, and US.

name: SecureBio
notes_head: Nonprofit biosecurity organization founded in 2022 by MIT professor Kevin Esvelt. Develops AI biosecurity benchmarks and evaluations cited by all major frontier labs (Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, OpenAI, xAI). Funded partly by effective altruism philanthropy.

name: Economic Security Action California
notes_head: State-level advocacy arm of Economic Security Project focused on California AI policy. Directed by Teri Olle. Co-sponsored SB 1047 (AI safety bill vetoed by Gov. Newsom) and SB 53 (signed into law, creating CalCompute public cloud infrastructure and establishing AI transparency/safety standards). Advocates for mandatory safety guardrails on frontier AI companies, whistleblower protections, and public computing access to democratize AI development. Part of the California Antimonopoly Table coalition. Takes a strong stance against tech industry self-regulation, arguing companies will not put pub

name: Information Technology Industry Council
notes_head: Major Washington DC-based tech industry trade association representing the world's largest technology companies. Premier global advocate for technology policy, with dedicated AI policy team. Developed an AI Accountability Framework and Global AI Policy Recommendations covering innovation, public trust, security/privacy, regulation, and global engagement. Supports risk-based, sector-specific AI regulation rather than broad restrictions; has argued lawmakers should limit regulation to avoid impeding industry growth. Advocates for federal preemption of state AI laws. Led coalition of 60+ organiza

name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
loc: San Francisco, CA (HQ at 182 Howard Street)
notes_head: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) is an American philanthropic advising and funding organization with CEO Alexander Berger. As of June 2025, Coefficient Giving has directed more than $4 billion in grants across various focus areas including global health, scientific research, pandemic preparedness, potential risks from advanced AI, and farm animal welfare. In November 2025, Open Philanthropy was renamed Coefficient Giving, reflecting its evolution from working primarily with one philanthropic partner to supporting multiple major donors pursuing cost-effective, high-impact giving.

name: Founders Fund
notes_head: Founders Fund is an American venture capital fund formed in 2005 and based in San Francisco, with roughly $17 billion in total assets under management as of 2025. The firm is closing in on a $6 billion fundraise for its fourth growth vehicle, with this rapid-fire pace happening less than a year after it wrapped up its previous $4.6 billion fund. Founders Fund was the first institutional investor in Space Exploration Technologies (SpaceX) and Palantir Technologies, and an early investor in Facebook. The firm's investments include Airbnb, Anduril, DeepMind, Rippling, Facebook, Ramp, Palantir Tec

name: Ergo Impact
loc: Location not specified in search results
notes_head: Ergo Impact appears to be referenced in multiple contexts across different sources. According to source, Ergo Impact is listed as a funding supporter for RAND's Geopolitics of AGI Initiative, alongside other donors like Good Ventures, Open Philanthropy, and Jaan Tallinn. Source indicates that Effective Giving rebranded to Ergo Impact. Source shows Ergo Impact operates as ergoimpact.org and describes itself as helping UHNW philanthropists find and fund solutions to pressing problems, laying the foundation for what became Ergo Impact.

name: Macroscopic Ventures
notes_head: Macroscopic Ventures is a Swiss nonprofit founded in 2019 that makes grants and investments to projects and people helping to build a future guided by reason and compassion. The organization employs 6 people and is headquartered in Basel, Switzerland, operating in 5 countries including United States, Japan, United Kingdom, and Canada. Their focus areas include artificial intelligence governance and policy, AI welfare, cooperative AI, societal long-term risks, and animal welfare. The organization was previously known as Center for Emerging Risk Research (CERR) and adopted its current name in Fe

name: Cooperative AI Foundation
notes_head: The Cooperative AI Foundation (CAIF) is a charitable entity backed by a $15 million philanthropic commitment from Macroscopic Ventures. CAIF's mission is to support research that will improve the cooperative intelligence of advanced AI for the benefit of all. The foundation is registered with the Charity Commission for England and Wales under charity number 1201294. CAIF employs 16 people with a 77.8% year-over-year growth.

name: Lionheart Ventures
loc: San Francisco Bay Area, CA (operates in 6 countries including UK, Costa Rica)
notes_head: Lionheart Ventures is an early-stage venture capital firm focused on the wise development of transformative technologies. Their main areas of focus are artificial intelligence and frontier mental health. The firm was founded in 2019 and is based in San Francisco. Their AI portfolio includes Softmax, Anthropic, Goodfire, Gray Swan, Piramidal, and Lucid Computing.

name: Meta Charity Funders
notes_head: Meta Charity Funders (MCF) is a funding circle that aims to fund charitable projects working one level removed from direct impact. MCF is a network of donors sharing knowledge, discussing funding opportunities, and running joint open grant rounds in the EA meta space. The organization received approximately 60 applications in Spring 2025 and ultimately funded 8 projects, with members granting approximately $530,000 in that round. Members are expected to give above $100,000 to this cause area each year, which is the expected minimum annual giving to join.

name: Mythos Ventures
notes_head: Mythos Ventures is an early-stage venture capital firm based in San Francisco, California, founded in 2023. The firm closed $14 million in capital commitments for its first fund to invest in transformative AI companies. Mythos Ventures announced Fund II as a $30M pre-seed fund backing founders building AGI-proof companies. The firm employs 5 people and is headquartered at 1410 Franklin St, San Francisco.

name: Juniper Ventures
loc: San Francisco, CA (also has Palo Alto office)
notes_head: Juniper Ventures is a venture capital firm that focuses on investing in startups that enhance the safety and security of artificial intelligence. The firm spun out of Climate Capital to invest in synthetic biology for the climate, with founding partners Michael Luciani and Jennifer Kan who had been investing in climate for years as part of Climate Capital. Their first fund is $10.6 million and is oversubscribed, with limited partners including family offices, foundations, and Allocator One serving as the anchor. The firm operates by writing checks between $100,000 and $500,000 to scientists wh

name: GiveWiki
notes_head: GiveWiki is described as "Your Crowdsourced Charity Evaluator" and "The AngelList for philanthropy" that allows users to follow expert grantmakers with their donations. The platform was formerly known as "Impact Markets" and was rebranded to GiveWiki in 2023. GiveWiki operates as a community-driven platform where users register their donations and the platform aggregates them into a list of top charities ranked by their community buy-in. The platform initially focused exclusively on AI safety projects but later reopened to all cause areas in 2024.

name: OpenBook
notes_head: OpenBook is a grants database tracking effective altruism funding, including substantial AI safety investments. Shows major donors like Open Philanthropy ($1.5B total), Jaan Tallinn (hundreds of millions to AI safety orgs like AI Impacts $546K, Fund for Alignment Research $524K), and recipient organizations across the AI safety ecosystem. No longer actively maintained according to AISafety.com map. Provides transparency into funding flows between major EA funders and AI safety research organizations.

name: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
notes_head: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) is a private American venture capital firm founded in 2009 by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz. The firm raised over $15 billion across multiple funds in 2025, representing over 18% of all venture capital dollars allocated in the United States that year. a16z has over $90B+ under management across multiple funds as of March 5, 2026. The firm focuses on investing across AI, bio + healthcare, consumer, crypto, enterprise, fintech, games, infrastructure, and companies building toward American dynamism.

name: Manifund
notes_head: Manifund is a platform for funding impactful projects that helps great charities get the funding they need. Manifund is a Philanthropic Fundraising Services company and is classified as a Nonprofit. The organization employs 4 people and is registered as a 501(c)3-nonprofit with EIN 88-3668801. In 2023, Manifund allocated $2m across dozens of charitable projects, primarily in AI safety and effective altruism cause areas.

name: Y Combinator
loc: Mountain View, CA (relocated to San Francisco Pier 70)
notes_head: Founded 2005, world's most prolific startup accelerator with 5,000+ portfolio companies valued at $1T+ collectively. Invests $500K in every company through standardized SAFE structure. Notable AI portfolio: OpenAI, Scale AI, Anthropic (indirectly). Current leadership: Garry Tan (President/CEO), Jared Friedman, Harj Taggar (Managing Partners). 92% of Fall 2025 batch (155+ companies) incorporated AI. Pioneered 'AI-native agencies' thesis breaking traditional VC rule against services businesses.

name: EA Long-Term Future Fund
loc: United Kingdom (hosted by Centre for Effective Altruism)
notes_head: Philanthropic fund making grants since 2017, originally called Far Future Fund. Has distributed over $20 million across hundreds of grants. Recent payout: $5.36M to 141 grantees (May 2023-March 2024). Fund managers include Caleb Parikh, Linchuan Zhang, Lawrence Chan, Oliver Habryka, and others with technical AI safety backgrounds. Spun out from Effective Ventures and distanced from Open Philanthropy since 2023.

name: Thrive Capital
loc: New York City, NY + London office
notes_head: Founded 2010 by Joshua Kushner, managing ~$50B in assets across multiple funds. Thrive X raised $10B in 2025/2026, largest fund yet. High-conviction strategy investing in ~12 companies annually with 'ride-or-die' approach. Major AI investments include $1B+ in OpenAI, led Isomorphic Labs $600M Series A, participated in Anthropic $3.5B round. Portfolio includes Stripe, SpaceX, Databricks, Anduril. Operates Thrive Holdings for AI-enabled services acquisitions.

name: Survival and Flourishing Fund
notes_head: Founded 2019, evolved from Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative. Distributed $152MM total, $34.33M in 2025 alone. Primary funder is Jaan Tallinn (Skype co-founder, ~$900M net worth). Uses unique 'S-process' algorithmic grant allocation with 6-12 independent recommenders. Second-largest AI safety funder after Open Philanthropy. Key programs include S-Process grants, Speculation Grants, and new Matching Pledge Program.

name: The Navigation Fund
notes_head: Founded 2023 by crypto billionaire Jed McCaleb ($3B net worth) and Seemay Chou (Arcadia Science CEO). $1.3 billion endowment, grew from $4.1M grants (2023) to $36.8M (2024). Owns 75% stake in Voltage Park ($883M valuation, 35,000+ Nvidia GPUs). Five focus areas: open science, farm animal welfare, criminal justice, climate, digital sentience. President David Coman-Hidy (former Humane League). Public charity structure accepts outside donations.

name: AI Alignment Foundation
notes_head: Philanthropic foundation funding neglected approaches to AI alignment through grants and fiscal sponsorship to independent researchers, educational programs, and engineers developing mechanistic interpretability tools. Conducts original research on AI introspection and self-steering. Has published work on teaching AI to read its own mind and AI resistance to steering. Advocates to defense agencies and government leaders on AI safety policy.

name: AI Risk Mitigation Fund
notes_head: Launched December 2023 as spinoff from Long-Term Future Fund. Led by Thomas Larsen with team of AI safety researchers and experienced grantmakers. Previously made $20+ million in grants under LTFF. Focus areas include technical AI alignment research, AI policy, and building research capacity. Past grants include $530K from SFF in 2024. Grantmaking process uses -5 to +5 scoring system with funding threshold around 2.5-3.0.

name: Astralis Foundation
loc: Stockholm, London, New York, Vienna (multi-city presence)
notes_head: European-based multi-donor foundation with $25M annual giving capacity. Raised >$20M from 15 donors, deployed to 14 organizations. Key initiative is $5.8M Shared Horizons Fund for international AI governance from 5 donors to 11 organizations. Leadership includes CEO Jona Glade and President Vilhelm Skoglund. Maintains strict donation acceptance policy excluding AI company leaders and government funding to preserve independence.

name: EA Infrastructure Fund
notes_head: Founded ~2017, part of EA Funds managed by Effective Ventures. Has distributed $18.9M+ since 2020 across 499 grants. Focuses on 'principles-first EA' supporting cause prioritization research, epistemic infrastructure, and EA community building. Fund managers include Harri Besceli, Jamie Harris, with advisors like Jonas Vollmer and Catherine Low. Recent grants include $168K to Rethink Priorities for cross-cause cost-effectiveness modeling.

name: Giving What We Can
notes_head: Founded 2009 by Toby Ord and William MacAskill. Community of 10,000+ pledgers who commit to donate 10% of income to effective charities. Annual revenue $3.2M, 36 employees. Has facilitated over $500M in total donations across causes including AI safety through recommended funds like EA Funds Long-Term Future Fund and Longview's Emerging Challenges Fund. BHAG goal: 1M pledgers donating $3B annually by 2040.

name: Long-Term Future Fund
notes_head: Founded 2017 as part of EA Funds program. Has distributed over $50M across 1,500+ grants since inception. Recent funding: $5.36M distributed May 2023-March 2024 across 141 grants with 19.3% acceptance rate. Fund managers include Caleb Parikh (chair), Linchuan Zhang, Oliver Habryka, Lawrence Chan. Makes primarily small grants (<$100k) to individuals and early-stage projects in AI safety, biosecurity, and longtermist research.

name: Halcyon Futures
notes_head: Founded 2023 by Mike McCormick (former GPV partner). Dual structure: Halcyon Futures (nonprofit making grants and career transition support) and Halcyon Ventures (VC arm). Recent $4.2M pre-seed raise (Jan 2026). Portfolio includes Goodfire, AIUC, Asymmetric Security, Confident Security. Has helped portfolio companies raise $300M+ in follow-on funding. Publishes quarterly AIUC-1 AI safety standard.

name: Science of Trustworthy AI (Schmidt Sciences)
notes_head: Major philanthropic program within Schmidt Sciences (founded 2024) by Eric and Wendy Schmidt. Offers grants up to $5M+ for 1-3 year projects across two tiers. Features advisory board including Percy Liang, Ajeya Cotra, and others. Provides compute resources, API credits, and engineering support. 2026 RFP deadline May 17, with emphasis on academic and nonprofit researchers addressing foundational AI safety science.

name: Catalyze Impact
notes_head: Catalyze Impact is a non-profit organization that incubates AI safety, security, and resilience organizations. The organization has incubated approximately 15 AI safety organizations to date, with the majority raising 6 or 7 figures in funding. Catalyze operates seed funding networks including a non-profit Seed Funding Circle for donors with $15k+ annual capacity and an Investor Network for VCs and angel investors with $20k+ annual capacity.

name: AI2050 (Schmidt Sciences)
notes_head: Launched in 2022 by Schmidt Sciences as a philanthropic initiative co-chaired by Eric Schmidt and James Manyika. Awards over $18M annually to researchers through fellowship program. Now has 99 fellows across 8 countries and 42 institutions in fourth cohort. Uses closed nomination process rather than open applications. Fellows have published 100+ papers in leading journals and created datasets like 'Humanity's Last Exam' AI benchmark.

name: Lightspeed Grants
notes_head: Fast-funding initiative run by Lightcone Infrastructure, primarily funded by Jaan Tallinn. Distributed $9.6M in 2023 round with grants ranging $5K-$5M. Applications processed in 14-60 days. Funded projects like Palisade Research ($401K), Center for AI Safety ($16K), and various individual researchers. Last active funding round was 2023.

name: Preamble Windfall Foundation
notes_head: 501(c)(3) founded around 2022, now defunct for unknown reasons. Founded by Jonathan Rodriguez Cefalu (Preamble Inc. founder, Stanford CS, ex-Snap). Funded research including SIMC4 wargame simulator for nuclear scenarios and AI red-teaming research. Filed Form 990-N indicating annual revenue under $50,000. Connected to broader Preamble ecosystem including commercial AI safety company Preamble Inc.

name: Superlinear Prizes
notes_head: Decentralized bounty platform for existential risk reduction and effective altruist cause areas. Offers prize money for delivering on specific AI safety projects. Listed as no longer accepting applications as of 2026. Part of the broader effective altruism ecosystem funding AI safety research through competition-based incentives.

name: Saving Humanity from Homo Sapiens (SHfHS)
notes_head: Small philanthropic foundation with long history in X-risk funding. Does not conduct direct research but identifies and financially supports individuals and organizations working on existential risk reduction. Part of broader AI safety funding ecosystem alongside organizations like Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Currently not accepting applications according to multiple sources.

name: AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund (AISTOF)
notes_head: Pooled multi-donor charitable fund managed by JueYan Zhang, former BlackRock portfolio manager and Founders Pledge investment committee chair. Raised over $10M since 2023 and committed 70+ grants. Structured for rapid deployment to capture emerging opportunities in AI safety landscape. Notable grantees include Luthien Research and other technical safety organizations. Praised by Open Philanthropy as filling important gaps in AI safety funding before major funders scaled up.

name: Longview Philanthropy
notes_head: Founded 2018 by Natalie Cargill. 30 employees, directed over $133M toward AI risk reduction as of March 2026. Manages Frontier AI Fund ($20M raised, $11.1M disbursed to 18 orgs), Digital Sentience Fund ($2.5M), and Emerging Challenges Fund. CEO Simran Dhaliwal, serves major donors giving >$100K with bespoke philanthropy strategies.

name: 5050 AI (by Fifty Years)
loc: San Francisco, CA + London, UK (partnership with ARIA)
notes_head: Free 13-week startup accelerator program run by Fifty Years VC, helping scientists and engineers build AI safety/alignment companies. 445 participants to date, launched 105 companies with 96% fundraising success rate. Mentors include OpenAI co-founder Wojciech Zaremba, former OpenAI interim CEO Emmett Shear. UK program launched in partnership with ARIA in 2025. No equity taken, entirely free program focused on commercializing AI safety research.

name: AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Pooled multi-donor fund structured to be fast and rapidly capture emerging opportunities, including in governance, technical alignment, and evaluations. Managed by JueYan Zhang.

name: AI2050
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Philanthropic initiative supporting researchers working on key opportunities and hard problems that are critical to get right for society to benefit from AI.

name: Science of Trustworthy AI
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Funder housing the science-focused philanthropic efforts of Eric and Wendy Schmidt, moving large amounts of funding towards AI safety via its AI institute.

name: Saving Humanity from Homo Sapiens
notes_head: From AISafety.com map. Small organization with a long history of finding the people doing the best work to prevent human-created existential risks and financially supporting them.

name: AI Grant
notes_head: Founded 2017 as non-profit distributed AI research lab. Provides $2.5K-$50K non-dilutive grants specifically for AI safety, interpretability, and alignment research. Made 104 investments with 10 exits including Common Sense Machines (acquired by Google). Operates as accelerator/incubator focused exclusively on AI startups.

name: SoftBank
notes_head: Founded 1981, manages $140+ billion through Vision Funds. Major AI investments include $64.6 billion in OpenAI (13% ownership), $1.4 billion in Skild AI, and leadership in the $500 billion Stargate AI infrastructure project. Portfolio includes 300+ AI and tech companies across foundation models, robotics, and infrastructure.

name: Khosla Ventures
notes_head: Founded 2004 by Sun Microsystems co-founder Vinod Khosla. Manages $16B AUM across 527+ portfolio companies. Early OpenAI investor (valued at $150B+). Investment focus includes AI, climate tech, healthcare with 'black swan' philosophy targeting contrarian opportunities. Recent major AI investments include Cognition Labs (Devin AI), Waabi ($750M Series C), and Emergent ($70M Series B).

name: Sequoia Capital
notes_head: Founded 1972, manages ~$85B in assets. Major AI investments include OpenAI, Anthropic, Reflection AI, and Ricursive Intelligence. Published influential AI market analyses including 'Generative AI's Act Two' and '2026: This is AGI' defining AGI as 'the ability to figure things out.' Portfolio companies account for 25% of NASDAQ's total value.

name: Greylock
notes_head: Founded 1965, one of Silicon Valley's most storied VC firms. Partners include Reid Hoffman (LinkedIn founder). Over 80% of investments are first checks (pre-seed to Series A). Explicit 'AI-first' strategy with major investments in Abnormal Security, Baseten, Adept AI, Cogent Security, and Resolve AI. Check sizes $300K-$10M across early stages.

name: Lux Capital
notes_head: Founded 2000, $7B AUM across 9 funds. Latest Fund IX raised $1.5B in January 2026. 200+ portfolio companies including major AI investments: Anduril ($30.5B valuation), Applied Intuition ($15B), Hugging Face, Runway AI, Cognition ($10.2B), Physical Intelligence. Strong focus on defense tech, robotics, and frontier science applications.

name: 8VC
notes_head: Founded 2015, approximately $7B AUM across six flagship funds. 363+ portfolio companies including 35 unicorns. Major AI investments include Palantir (co-founded by partner Joe Lonsdale), AI21 Labs, Atomic AI, Cognition AI. Strong focus on 'Smart Enterprise' thesis applying AI to traditional industries like healthcare, logistics, and government.

name: Slow Ventures
notes_head: Founded 2011, generalist early-stage VC firm with ~$1B deployed across 500+ companies. Led by Sam Lessin and Kevin Colleran (Facebook alums). Notable exits include Slack, Robinhood, Airtable, Solana. Recently raised $275M across Fund VI and Opportunity Fund III (2024). Portfolio spans fintech, SaaS, consumer, healthcare, crypto with $250K-$3M check sizes primarily at seed stage.

name: Entrepreneur First
notes_head: Founded 2011, operates globally with offices in London, Paris, Singapore, New York, and San Francisco. Recently raised $200M at unicorn valuation with portfolio worth over $16B. Notable AI investments include Gensyn, Tractable (unicorn), Photoroom, and numerous other AI startups. Runs specialized AI Startup School program and moves all funded companies to Bay Area for scaling.

name: Plural
notes_head: Founded 2021, employs 22 people. Led by partners including Taavet Hinrikus, Khaled Helioui, and Ian Hogarth. Recent AI investments include $10.25M in Callosum (AI compute orchestration), investments in Helsing (defense AI), Hypersonica (hypersonics), and Upside Robotics (agricultural AI). Fund II has $30.3M total value with 50.19% gross IRR.

name: Schmidt Futures
notes_head: Founded 2017 by Eric and Wendy Schmidt, transitioned into Schmidt Sciences in 2024. 164 employees across 36 countries. Major AI investments include backing Anthropic, Cloudline ($6M), and Gradium ($70M). Operates AI2050 fellowship program and Science of Trustworthy AI initiative. Spun out Ex/ante investment fund in 2023 with $33M inaugural fund.

name: Gates Foundation
notes_head: The Gates Foundation is an American private foundation founded by Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates, based in Seattle, Washington, launched in 2000 and reported to be the third-wealthiest charitable foundation in the world, holding $86 billion in assets as of July 31, 2025. The foundation's primary stated goals are to enhance healthcare and reduce extreme poverty across the world, and to expand educational opportunities and access to information technology in the United States. In May 2025, Bill Gates announced the foundation will spend $200 billion over the next 20 years and set a new date

name: Emerson Collective
notes_head: Emerson Collective, LLC is an American company founded by Laurene Powell Jobs in 2011 in Palo Alto, California. The company invests in entrepreneurs and innovators working in education, energy and the environment, immigration, media and journalism. Emerson Collective has invested in at least nine AI startups since 2022, with involvement in AI funding rounds totaling over $1 billion. The organization operates as a limited liability company and focuses on venture capital investment and philanthropy.

name: AngelList
notes_head: AngelList is building the infrastructure that powers the startup economy and aims to accelerate innovation by providing startups and investors with the connected tools they need to launch and scale a startup or fund—and invest in both. AngelList reaches over $171B in assets on the platform, with over 72k investors, 13k active startups, and more than 25k funds and syndicates supported. AngelList is the parent company of AngelList Venture, AngelList Talent, and Product Hunt and has more than 20 unicorns in the portfolio. The company provides investors and innovators with the tools to grow, offer

name: BlackRock
notes_head: BlackRock is a global asset manager and technology provider with over $14 trillion in assets under management as of Q4 2025. The company has formed an AI Infrastructure Partnership with Microsoft, raising $12.5 billion toward a $30 billion goal. BlackRock has built an AI research platform dubbed 'Asimov' for use in its fundamental equity business. The company's CEO Larry Fink has framed AI as 'the most significant technology since, at least, the computer' in his 2026 annual letter to investors.

name: Element AI
notes_head: Element AI was a Canadian multinational artificial intelligence company based in Montreal, Quebec that was founded in October 2016. The company was funded by the Government of Canada for CAD$5 million, and raised US$102 million independently, before being acquired by ServiceNow. ServiceNow announced it has signed an agreement to acquire Element AI on November 30, 2020, with ServiceNow completing the acquisition of Element AI on January 8, 2021. The company was running out of money and options and sold for US $230 million.

name: Inovia Capital
notes_head: Inovia Capital is a leading Canadian multi-stage venture capital firm managing over US$2.2B with offices in Montreal, Toronto, Calgary, San Francisco, and London. The firm actively invests in AI-native companies, with particular focus on generative AI, security, digital health, and commerce. In partnership with Mila, Inovia launched the Venture Scientist Fund, a ~$100M early-stage VC fund designed to transform frontier AI research from Canadian academic institutions into AI-native companies, targeting ~55+ investments. The firm has made 373+ investments spanning pre-seed to pre-IPO.

name: Ballmer Group
notes_head: The Ballmer Group is a philanthropy founded in 2015 by Steve and Connie Ballmer (Steve is the former CEO of Microsoft). In July 2025, the Ballmer Group co-launched NextLadder Ventures, a $1 billion, 15-year initiative alongside the Gates Foundation, Stand Together, and Valhalla Foundation to develop AI tools for frontline public-sector workers — including public defenders, parole officers, and social workers — to expand economic opportunity for low-income Americans. NextLadder Ventures specifically employs AI tools developed by Anthropic. The Ballmer Group also integrates AI into its own inter

name: Craig Newmark Philanthropies
notes_head: Philanthropy founded by Craigslist creator Craig Newmark, focused on cybersecurity, trustworthy journalism, voter protection, and tech safety. Committed $100M+ to a Cyber Civil Defense network. Major funder of cybersecurity infrastructure including $3.2M to IST's UnDisruptable27 initiative protecting critical infrastructure from nation-state adversaries. Also funds UC Berkeley Public Interest Cybersecurity Program (extended through 2028), Global Cyber Alliance, and community cybersecurity clinics. Promotes 'Take 9' cyber-hygiene awareness campaign.

name: In-Q-Tel
notes_head: In-Q-Tel (IQT) is an independent nonprofit venture capital firm that invests in technology companies to equip the CIA and other intelligence agencies with cutting-edge capabilities. Founded in 1999, based in Tysons, Virginia. Reached its 800th investment milestone in April 2025. Total estimated investment exceeds $1.8B over 25+ years. Key AI/national-security portfolio companies include Palantir (early investor). IQT appears in 32 companies on the 2025 NatSec 100 Report — more than any other fund. Invests across AI, biotech, microchips, space systems, cybersecurity, and autonomous systems. Als

name: AI Fund
notes_head: AI Fund is Andrew Ng's venture studio, launched in 2018, that co-founds AI startups from scratch. Closed an oversubscribed $190M Fund II in 2025 (following $175M Fund I). Has co-founded ~35 portfolio companies including LandingAI and Workera. Corporate LPs include AES Corporation, HP Inc., Mitsui, Mitsubishi, QBE, and TELUS; VC investors include Sequoia Capital and NEA. AI Fund actively participates in strategy, coding, and customer discovery alongside CEOs. Focus areas: application and software infrastructure layers of the AI stack, leveraging foundation models across financial services, rene

name: MGX investment group
notes_head: Abu Dhabi state-owned AI investment vehicle launched March 2024 by Mubadala and G42, targeting $100B+ in AUM. Key partnerships: Stargate Project (with OpenAI, SoftBank, Oracle, Jan 2025); Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership (with BlackRock, Microsoft, Sep 2024). Acquired Aligned Data Centers for $40B (Oct 2025). Exploring $50B third-party capital raise. Also invested in Anthropic Series G (Feb 2026) and made $2B minority investment in Binance (Mar 2025).

name: Macquarie Asset Management
notes_head: Global alternative asset manager (part of Macquarie Group). Major AI infrastructure investor: committed $17B+ to data center and HPC investments including Applied Digital ($900M+ with $4.1B pipeline) and Aligned Data Centers (sold at $40B enterprise value to BlackRock/MGX consortium, Oct 2025). Closed Macquarie Infrastructure Partners VI with $8B+ in commitments (Jun 2025). Views AI as key driver of infrastructure demand in 2026 outlook.

name: Data Collective
notes_head: DCVC (formerly Data Collective) is a deep tech venture capital firm founded by Matt Ocko and Zachary Bogue, investing in entrepreneurs using computational approaches—especially AI—to solve large-scale real-world problems. Manages billions in AUM across 13 funds including six flagship funds. Invests at seed, Series A, and growth stages across AI, exascale computing, climate, robotics, materials science, biology, defense, and security. Backed by a network of 50+ technology executives and experts (CTOs, chief scientists, professors at Stanford and Berkeley). Was a lead investor in Element AI. Foc

name: Real Ventures
notes_head: Canada's leading early-stage venture capital firm, established in 2007 and headquartered in Montreal. Manages $325M across five funds with an active portfolio of 100+ companies valued at ~$10B. Has invested in 214 companies total, with portfolio unicorns including Xanadu, Blockstream, and Tenstorrent. Deeply embedded in Montreal's AI and deep tech ecosystem. Was an early investor/backer of Element AI. Focuses on nurturing entrepreneurial communities and supporting founders from the earliest stages. Portfolio spans AI, healthcare, fintech, and other technology verticals. Sonder (portfolio compa

name: Public Investment Fund
notes_head: Saudi Arabia sovereign wealth fund (~$930B AUM). Launched Humain (May 2025) as full-stack AI company — data centers, cloud, models (ALLAM Arabic LLM), applications. $40B AI investment fund. Strategic partnership with NVIDIA (500MW GPU capacity over 5 years). $10B joint investment with Google Cloud for AI hub. Aramco acquired minority stake in Humain. Central to Vision 2030 economic diversification. Chaired by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.

name: Ford Foundation
notes_head: Major US philanthropy (est. 1936) with significant AI accountability and governance funding. Part of $200M+ coalition (with MacArthur, Omidyar, Mozilla, Open Society Foundations) for public-interest AI. Co-launched Humanity AI, a $500M five-year initiative (Oct 2025) with ten major foundations. Funds AI Now Institute. Supports efforts on AI racial/social bias accountability, labor rights in AI deployment, and global AI fairness standards through Current AI collaboration. Focus areas: transparency, algorithmic accountability, worker protections.

name: Thrive Holdings
notes_head: AI-focused holding company launched April 2025 by Joshua Kushner's Thrive Capital. Buys, owns, and runs companies positioned to benefit from AI — starting with accounting (Crete Professionals Alliance) and IT services (Shield Technology Partners). OpenAI took ownership stake in Thrive Holdings (Dec 2025) and embeds engineering/product teams within portfolio companies. Parent firm Thrive Capital manages $25B+, raised $10B in 2026. Named TIME100 AI 2025 (Kushner). Early OpenAI investor ($130M in 2022, $1B at $285B valuation). Also invested in Databricks, Anduril, Cursor/Anysphere.

name: William and Flora Hewlett Foundation
notes_head: Major U.S. philanthropic foundation ($13.9B assets as of Dec 2024, $631M in grants in 2024). Significant funder of AI governance, cyber policy, and responsible technology through its Cyber Initiative ($150M over 10 years) and Special Projects program. Funded CSET at Georgetown ($3M+), Hoover Institution Technology Policy Accelerator ($2.5M), Vanderbilt AI information warfare research ($2.5M), and multiple HBCU/MSI cyber policy programs ($20M+). Co-convener of PIT-UN alongside Ford Foundation and New America. One of the largest philanthropic funders of AI governance infrastructure in the U.S. H

name: MacArthur Foundation
notes_head: Major U.S. philanthropy (est. 1978, ~$7B endowment). Part of Humanity AI, a $500M five-year initiative ensuring AI is shaped by and for people; committed $10M in aligned AI grantmaking. Technology in the Public Interest program funds AI governance, auditing, and accountability research. Granted $2M each to AI Now Institute and Brookings AI Initiative. Overarching goal: strengthen democratic oversight and innovation in AI governance through evaluation, auditing, and accountability mechanisms centering public interest. Three-pronged strategy: (1) strengthen research base for AI evaluations; (2)

name: B Capital
notes_head: Global venture capital firm co-founded in 2015 by Eduardo Saverin (Facebook co-founder) and Raj Ganguly. Over $9B in AUM across 9 offices and 100+ employees. Invests seed through late-stage in enterprise software, AI/ML, fintech, healthcare tech, and security. Led Goodfire's $150M Series B at $1.25B valuation. Saverin views AI as "a foundational capability that amplifies everything we do." Active across US and Southeast Asia markets.

name: Menlo Ventures
notes_head: Major Silicon Valley venture capital firm investing across AI, consumer, enterprise, and healthcare. Raised $1.35B for AI-era investments. Portfolio includes 466 companies, 25 unicorns, 20 IPOs (including Uber, Roku). Key AI investments: Anthropic, Pinecone, Goodfire. Led Goodfire's $50M Series A. Created the $100M Anthology Fund with Anthropic to fund AI companies. Actively invests across the AI stack from infrastructure/models to applications.

name: LTFF
notes_head: Long-Term Future Fund (LTFF) — one of four funds managed by Effective Ventures under EA Funds. Supports projects working to positively influence the long-term trajectory of civilization, with AI safety/alignment research as its largest funding category (~2/3 of grants). Granted $6.67M total in 2023 (~$4.3M on AI safety). Median AI safety grant is $25k (vs. Open Phil's $257k median), making it well-suited for individuals, upskilling, and career transitions. Distinctive for funding unconventional projects and acting as a funder of last resort. Applications reviewed by committee of grantmakers wi

name: Good Ventures
notes_head: Philanthropic foundation co-founded by Dustin Moskovitz and Cari Tuna; primary funder of Open Philanthropy (rebranded Coefficient Giving, Nov 2025). Accounts for >50% of philanthropic AI safety funding globally; has distributed $5B+ in grants including $5.5M to launch CHAI at UC Berkeley.

name: Metaplanet
notes_head: Venture capital and philanthropy vehicle founded in 2011 by Skype co-founding engineer Jaan Tallinn, based in Tallinn, Estonia. Operates as a hybrid venture-philanthropy investing on decades-to-centuries timelines in deep tech addressing existential risks, especially advanced AI. Has invested in 184+ companies, primarily at Seed and Series A stages, with 11 unicorns in portfolio. Tallinn was a Series A investor and former board member at DeepMind, led the Series A for Anthropic (now board observer), and co-founded the Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (CSER) and the Future of Life Insti

name: Delphi Ventures
notes_head: Crypto and Web3-focused venture capital firm launched in 2020. Thesis-driven, high-conviction fund investing in blockchain, DeFi, and crypto-AI intersection. Notable portfolio companies include Axie Infinity, Uniswap, dYdX, Celestia, Solana, Lido, and zkSync. Has invested in 98+ companies. Co-launched an AI accelerator with the NEAR Foundation. Invested in Mythos Ventures (an AI venture capital fund). Note: distinct from the older Delphi Ventures life sciences VC (founded 1988, San Mateo).

name: The Operating Group
notes_head: Small venture capital fund based in Road Town, British Virgin Islands, investing primarily in Seed-stage blockchain and crypto startups. Has invested in 5 companies including Mythos Ventures. Team of 2 partners. Limited public information available.

name: Cisco Investments
notes_head: Corporate venture investment arm of Cisco Systems, founded 1993, based in San Jose, CA. Launched a $1B AI investment fund in June 2024. Has invested in Cohere, Mistral AI, Scale AI, and World Labs among others. Over 30 years of investing in hundreds of companies across Cisco's core markets and strategic areas. Over 20 AI-focused acquisitions and investments in recent years. Uses build-buy-partner-invest-co-develop strategy. Has 8 funds including dedicated Cisco AI Investment Fund. Strategic investor in xAI.

name: DST Global
notes_head: Global venture capital and private equity firm founded in 2009 by Yuri Milner, spun off from Russian company Digital Sky Technologies (now Mail.ru/VK). Primarily invests in late-stage internet and technology companies. Major portfolio includes Facebook, Twitter, WhatsApp, Snapchat, Spotify, Alibaba, ByteDance. Invested in SSI $1B Series A (Sept 2024) alongside Sequoia, a16z, SV Angel. Has invested in 80+ companies across multiple funds. Milner renounced Russian citizenship in 2022; DST Global funds have no Russia-based investments. Also connected to Breakthrough Foundation (science philanthrop

name: SV Angel
notes_head: Iconic Silicon Valley seed-stage venture capital firm founded in 2009 by Ron Conway, known as the "Godfather of Silicon Valley." Made early bets on Google, Facebook, Twitter, Airbnb. Raised six funds through 2018, then briefly returned to angel investing before raising a $269M growth equity fund in 2022. Currently managed by Ron Conway and sons Topher and Ronny Conway. Invested in SSI $1B Series A (Sept 2024) alongside Sequoia, a16z, DST Global. Portfolio of nearly 300 companies through 2011. Focus on early-stage technology companies that cannot secure traditional VC funding.

name: Greenoaks Capital
notes_head: San Francisco-based growth equity investment firm founded in 2012. Known for extremely concentrated bets (~15 companies per fund, $25-75M initial checks). Closed $2.5B Fund VI (beating $2.25B target). Led SSI $2B funding round (2025, $32B valuation) alongside a16z, Lightspeed, and DST Global. Greenoaks reportedly invested $500M in that round. Focus areas include AI, cybersecurity, fintech, and vertical SaaS. Emphasizes long-term, high-conviction investing with active governance. Also invested in Scale AI and Anthropic.

name: Protocol Labs
notes_head: Innovation network founded by Juan Benet (2014) connecting 600+ tech startups, funds, accelerators, and foundations. Created IPFS and Filecoin.
